results.webTitle: Politics live: readers' edition - Friday 11 October

fields.headline: Politics live: readers' edition - Friday 11 October
fields.trailText: Share breaking news, leave links to interesting articles online and chat about the day's political events in our open thread
fields.bodyText: I'm not writing my usual Politics Live blog today, but, as an alternative, here's Politics Live: readers' edition. It's intended to be a place where you can catch up with the latest news and find links to good politics blogs and articles on the web. Please feel free to use this as somewhere you can comment on any of the day's political stories - just as you do when I'm writing the daily blog. It would be particularly useful for readers to flag up new material in the comments - breaking news or blogposts or tweets that are worth passing on because someone is going to find them interesting. A lot of what I do on my blog is aggregation - finding the good stuff and passing it on - and you can do this, too (as I know, because it happens every day when I'm blogging). All of today's Guardian politics stories are here, and all the politics stories filed on Thursday including some in today's paper, are here.

fields.headline: Jack Straw: a life in politics – in pictures
fields.trailText: A look back at the Labour politician's career, which included stints as foreign secretary and home secretary

results.webTitle: Politics as it happened - Canberra reacts to Indonesia crisis

fields.headline: Politics as it happened - Canberra reacts to Indonesia crisis
fields.trailText: Australia's relationship with Indonesia sinks with ambassador recalled as fallout continues over spying allegations. Follow all the latest with politics live
fields.bodyText: It has been a weird day down this end of the press gallery. It has been characterised by the fallout from the Guardian/ABC's Indonesia allegations, scrambled by Senate estimates committees, party room meetings in the Coalition and caucus, Clive Palmer, Christine Milne, Bob Katter and capped off by Kamahl. It is not too much of a stretch to say your correspondent needed Five Eyes to keep on top of it. But let's be methodical. The Indonesian president spoke out for the first time on allegations that his phone and that of his wife had been tapped by Australian intelligence agencies. SBY said Tony Abbott responded too "lightly" and promptly recalled the Indonesian ambassador. Tony Abbott said the government always acted in the national interest and "others" should not expect anything else. He again underlined the importance of the Australian Indonesian relationship. Finance minister Mathias Cormann and former finance minister Penny Wong sparred over who was guilty of the biggest job cuts in the public service. Notwithstanding the Coalition commitment to cut 12,000 through natural attrition, the government will refer the whole lot to the Commission of Audit for advice. Clive Palmer urged Treasurer Joe Hockey not to allow Archer Daniels Midland to takeover GrainCorp and Labor members expressed their concerns about the sale in the party room, even though shadow treasurer Chris Bowen has supported the sale. Operation Sovereign Borders chief Angus Campbell revealed that he had come up with the media strategy of weekly briefings, rather than immigration minister Scott Morrison. Immigration officials also confirmed that the term "illegals" had been instituted across the department after notification by Morrison. Until tomorrow, good night. Meanwhile in Senate estimates, Martin Bowles, immigration department secretary is very nervous about talking about individual cases of young children. We operate in the best interests of all the people in the detention system. I know it makes no sense at all but just so you know, the entertainer Kamahl, has visited Parliament to recite Abraham Lincoln's Gettysburg address to mark its 150th anniversary. Why? Not sure but Barnaby Joyce was there. Immigration officials confirm there are eight pregnant women on Nauru among asylum seekers. Martin Bowles, immigration department secretary is saying generally the policy is not to separate families but it depends on the circumstances.. Campbell has confirmed OSB is not trying to purchase fishing boats "at this time". He defines an "operational matter" for the benefit of Senator Kim Carr. Those issues that might prejudice the conduct of current and future operations. That is, anything that: Provides an advantage to people smugglers Provides information to allow the manipulation of passengers. Has the potential to damage bilateral relations Compromises the safety of our people. Martin Bowles, secretary of immigration, says the PNG solution has been "effective" and it has been "part of the the solution" which has seen a drop in numbers of asylum seeker arrivals. Parliament has moved into hyperdrive for the afternoon.... A division to gag the debate tomorrow (Wednesday) afternoon on the Mineral Resource Rent Tax repeal bills has been won by the government 80 - 54. In Senate, Angus Campbell is telling Greens senator Hanson-Young that he, not his minister Scott Morrison, came up with the media strategy which sees the careful control of information on arrivals. Chairman of the National Broadband Network Ziggy Switkowski is answering questions from Labor's Kate Lundy on the limitations (or not) of the Telstra copper system. Oh and Kamahl is in the building. Carr asks Angus Campbell how he feels about being involved in the political process? Campbell: I am not involved in the political process. My job is to provide confidential advice to the government of the day and to implement those policies of the government. General Campbell used the example of the recent Canadian mounted police operation to break a global pedophile ring. That was not reported on an incidental basis, says Campbell. In estimates, Senator Kim Carr asks the head of Operation Sovereign Borders, Angus Campbell, about an alleged incident in which a customs boat sought to tow a vessel and ripped the bow out of the vessel last Friday night. Labor also raised the incident in question time. Campbell: I would respectfully offer that it is not appropriate to be giving incidental advice of arrivals or on-water. Carr: The sinking of a vessel by an Australian vessel, the rescuing of 40 asylum seekers, could be considered a serious incident. Campbell says he makes recommendations to minister Scott Morrison about revealing information on serious incidents. There is a sound basis for not discussing events that occur on water. A series of tweets by the Indonesian president in English earlier today. Lieutenant-General Angus Campbell, head of the government's Operation Sovereign Borders, is up in front of the Senate estimates committee. He is explaining his information protocols. Katharine Murphy was watching the ABC managing director Mark Scott in the Senate estimates as he was questioned about the publication of revelations which exposed Australia’s attempts to spy on Indonesia. Scott faced questions during a senate estimates hearing in Canberra on Tuesday which required him to explain why the national broadcaster allowed the publication of documents with a top secret security classification. Scott was asked by the Liberal senator Anne Ruston whether any money had changed hands concerning the stories that were published simultaneously by both outlets on Monday. “No, certainly not,” he said. He said there was nothing unusual in the ABC collaborating with other media outlets on investigations - and he pointed to the Guardian’s policy of collaborating with other respected media partners around the world on the Snowden revelations. Joint ventures for the ABC, Scott said, were “not atypical.” Scott said Guardian Australia had supplied the documents to the ABC, and the broadcaster had embarked on an independent investigation. The ABC had sought legal advice, the stories were reported independently, and were published as soon as practicable, he said. He suggested the ABC was able to offer Guardian Australia reporting depth and a broadcast platform. Labor is trying to pin down the assistant education minister Sussan Ley on benefits around childcare, ahead of the findings of the Productivity Commission. Bill Shorten attempts to elicit a promise from Abbott not to cut childcare payments over the next term of government. We will fully honour our election commitments, says Abbott. Question time is over and the Opposition shadow Kate Ellis is now speaking on childcare as a Matter of Public Importance. Re my earlier comment on the plural of booby, thanks to Brian Dodd. Communications minister Malcolm Turnbull is in full flight, taking the mickey out of the National Broadband Network. Turnbull describes the visits to the Northern Territory by Labor members, including the member for chiefly Ed Husic and the former minister Stephen Conroy. Turnbull says Husic employed his "powerful charisma" to switch on the NBN yet in that territory, only 322 houses are capable of getting a broadband service and only five have connected. Conroy went to the Northern Territory more often than there are active connections. The Northern Territory News should have the number of connections on the front page. They like UFOs and crocodiles. What is a bigger crock than connections to the NBN. At it's current rate, it is about $2m per connection. The Prime Minister is asked by Chris Bowen about the special dividend of $8.8bn given to the Reserve Bank. Abbott says the government topped up the capital of the Reserve Bank because capital had been stripped by the former government. Morrison is talking about his visit to an asylum seeker family in Townsville who "came the right way", waiting in a UN camp "for decades". They have established a home and educated their kids. Morrison is making the point that of the Coalition's 13,750 places under the special humanitarian program - not one will go to those "who arrive illegally by boat". Labor, he says, increased the humanitarian program to 20,000, of which 7000 were going to be protection visas for those who came by boat. Those who come the right way can expect the generous spirit and heart of the Australian people. Shorten asks the prime minister whether he stood buy the asylum seeker boat buy back scheme? Abbott says he stands by all election commitments. Education minister Christopher Pyne is talking about the public service job cuts. This was one of the booby traps left by the boobies opposite for the government. Spelling note: unsure of the plural of booby. The government's not-so-secret weapon, Joe "hasta la vista" Hockey, has been unleashed against Labor on the public service job cuts. The Coalition has promised 12,000 job cuts but say Labor's previous cuts were causing 14,500 cuts. This is about nailing the former Labor government for redundancies as a result of their efficiency dividend. The Coalition is getting the Commission of Audit to look at the matter. The PM's office has released the full statement made by Tony Abbott on Indonesia at the start of question time. In the past 24 hours there have been calls for Australia to detail our intelligence operations and to apologise for them. Madam Speaker, the first duty of every government is to protect the country and to advance its national interests. That’s why every government gathers information and why every government knows that every other government gathers information. Madam Speaker, there is no greater responsibility for a prime minister than ensuring the safety of Australian citizens and the security our borders and that, indeed, is why we do collect intelligence. National security, Madam Speaker, requires a consistent determination to do what’s best for Australia and that’s why this government will support the national security decisions of previous ones as we will expect future governments to respect ours. Madam Speaker, Australia should not be expected to apologise for the steps we take to protect our country now or in the past, any more than other governments should be expected to apologise for the similar steps that they have taken. Importantly, in Australia’s case, we use all our resources, including information, to help our friends and allies, not to harm them. Similarly, Madam Speaker, Australia shouldn’t be expected to detail what we do to protect our country any more than other governments should be expected to detail what they do to protect theirs. Others should ask of us no more than they are prepared to do themselves. Madam Speaker, I want to make it absolutely crystal clear that Australia has deep respect for Indonesia, for its government and for its people. I regard President Yudhoyono as a good friend of Australia, indeed as one of the very best friends that we have anywhere in the world. That’s why, Madam Speaker, I sincerely regret any embarrassment that recent media reports have caused him. But Madam Speaker, it is in everyone’s interests – Indonesia’s no less than Australia’s – that cool heads prevail and that our relationship grows closer, not more distant. I pledge myself to build the strongest possible relationship with Indonesia. After all, due to its size, proximity and potential to be an emerging democratic superpower of Asia, it is the most important single relationship that we have. Speaker Bishop mistakenly asks the member for Hunter "Joel" Fitzgibbon to sit down. "What's going on here?" jokes Treasurer Joe Hockey, regarding the special relationship between Fitzgibbon and the Speaker, who is supposed to refer to his title: the member for Hunter. General jocularity erupts. Warren Truss gets his turn to answer a question on the evils of the carbon tax. Marles asking Morrison about the effectiveness of the Iranian visa change in Indonesia and can he assure the House it will continue. I blinked and missed the answer as the minister sat down immediately. Independent Bob Katter asks how Abbott would feel if he and his wife's phones were tapped. Katter says "surely an apology is necessary" to the Indonesian president. Abbott says he is not going to support every aspect of previous government's foreign policy, saying he deplores the Oceanic Viking stand off and live export ban. But he is not going to be critical in respect of intelligence gathering. The prime minister says he does not believe Australia should be expected to apologise for "reasonable intelligence gathering operations". Scott Morrison in answer to Marles: The government works cooperatively with Indonesia (to stop people smuggling) in all measures. Tony Abbott earlier: Australia should not be expected to apologise for the steps we take to protect our country now or in the past anymore than other governments should be expected to apologise for the similar steps that they have taken. Importantly in Australia's case, we use all our resources including information to help our friends and allies and not to harm them. Australia shouldn't be expected to detail what we do to protect our country any more than other countries should be expected to detail what they do to protect theirs. Others should ask of us no more than they are prepared to do themselves. Richard Marles to Scott Morrison: were all those on board (towed boat) rescued, where were they taken and where are they now? Morrison: I've answered this question, I know the Opposition would like us to hand over the pin number to Operation Sovereign Borders. Labor is championing the interests of people smugglers, says Morrison. Labor's Tony Burke has asked for the imputation to be withdrawn. Morrison withdraws. More from Tony Abbott earlier at the start of question time on Indonesia: National security requires a consistent determination to do what's best for Australia and that's why this government will support the decisions of previous ones and expects future governments to respect ours. Dixer to Tony Abbott on the advantages to Australia of scrapping the carbon tax. Question one to Scott Morrison from Richard Marles who asks him to confirm last Friday a customs vessel attempted to tow an asylum seeker boat and it "broke up". Morrison says the government will not be supporting the Opposition's campaign for the the people smugglers right to know. More from Tony Abbott: The first duty of every government is to protect the country and advance its interests, that's why every government gathers information and why every government knows every other government gathers information. There is no greater responsibility for a prime minister than ensuring the safety of Australian citizens and the security of our borders and that indeed is why we do collect intelligence. At the start of question time, Tony Abbott has expressed his sincere regret for any embarrassment caused to Indonesia over spying allegations. He says it is the government's first duty to act in the national interest and says it's important for "cool heads to prevail". Abbott says the Australian government should not be expected to detail what it does to protect Australian interests and "others" should not expect them to. Bill Shorten says no one side of politics owns the relationship with Indonesia and calls on the government "not to allow this matter to fester". ABC managing director Mark Scott is before Senate estimates, saying he put Indonesian spying allegations to air as soon as practical. Regarding the partnership with The Guardian, Scott says the ABC has worked with Fairfax and News Corp on stories in the past. SA Liberal Senator Anne Ruston asked: is it reasonable to publish something with Top Secret written on it? Scott says the story was in the public interest and goes to the nature and extent of how intelligence gathering activities are undertaken and how material is shared. He says the discussion is similar to debates in the US after Edward Snowden released the documents. Scott made the comparison with Wheat Board scandal which also attracted criticism that the story was against the national interest. The Coalition party room has also met this morning. The prime minister told his troops it had been a good two weeks for the new government - getting on with business and "intelligently responding to the exingencies of the day." (A spokesman later clarified that the exigencies of the day included, in Abbott's account, the small matter of Indonesia.) Labor by contrast, Abbott reported, was consumed with self referential nonsense - determining who was the worst prime minister - Kevin Rudd or Julia Gillard - a "doleful duo." (Just for those interested, synonyms for doleful include wretched, forlorn, woebegone.) The prime minister reminded his marginal seats holders their first duty was to their electorate. He also praised colleagues, and further, encouraged them, to engage with ethnic communities. People needed to feel part of the family, Abbott reasoned. A number of colleagues took the opportunity of the meeting to praise Abbott for his deft handling of the issue of human rights whilst in Sri Lanka recently. The prime minister, by way of reply, noted he was not one of those politicans inclined to lecture neighbours on human rights; and he had no interest in taking sides in foreign conflicts. Abbott made a passing allusion to the spirit of Cardinal Moran - but if I go there in detail, it wil require a large digression that I won't quite pull off given the proximity of Question Time. Back to the questions. Liberal Andrew Nikolic made a big pitch for a defence centre at Scottsdale to deal with not only defence provisions but also food aid in the event of natural disasters. This, apparently, went down well. There was a negative reference to a looming documentary from John Pilger (described in the briefing as "that awful John Pilger") that would apparently make a negative contribution to the cause of constitutional recognition of Indigenous Australians. There was concern about the housing crisis in Sydney that was triggered by green regulations locking up land. And GrainCorp got a brief mention: one MP noted that lazy journalists were framing the whole transaction as Liberals versus Nationals. Foolishness. This was a matter of grain seats versus metropolitan seats. (Fair cop, that analysis.) This has been Katharine Murphy, for politicslive .. Labor senator Kim Carr asks for an explanation of the removal of the former secretary Andrew Metcalfe. Assistant immigration minister Michaelia Cash takes the question on notice. What's in a name? It cost $195,000 for the name change of the department from Immigration and Citizenship to Immigration and Border Protection. Martin Bowles, secretary of immigration department, will be answering questions on Operation Sovereign Borders any minute now. He is now giving his opening address to Senate estimates. Finance minister Mathias Cormann, says he has just discovered that Labor had planned job cuts to the tune of 14,500 jobs in the Australian public service. As a result, Cormann says the government has to reassess and will consider advice from Commission of Audit but they remain committed to achieving the committed 12,000 cuts through natural attrition. He says Labor only funded redundancies for some 800 jobs. Greens leader Christine Milne and fellow senator Scott Ludlam are speaking about diplomatic incidents including the Australian-Indonesian spy allegations, the jailing of Greenpeace activist Colin Russell in Russia and the gifting of two patrol boats to Sri Lanka. Milne says Tony Abbott's response to the Indonesian spying allegations, "trying to brush it away is only going to escalate matters". Acknowledge the (spying) mistake, it was clearly the last government and it won't happen again. Hello all, Katharine Murphy back again. (Quite liking this roving live blog correspondent gig, just quietly.) It being Tuesday, the Labor caucus has gathered in Canberra. The Labor leader Bill Shorten gave the troops a gee up - this new Coalition government was secretive and arrogant and not what they told the public they were pre-election. There were 'well dones' to Tony Burke, Richard Marles, and Chris Bowen - settling into post election roles. Legislation was considered. Questions were posed, including about the issue of the moment - GrainCorp. Shadow treasurer Chris Bowen has been trying to give the treasurer Joe Hockey some help recently about the controversial GrainCorp takeover. Bowen has been talking up the general national interest associated with foreign investment. Labor colleagues, however, seem less convinced. Bowen took some questions this morning from Labor folks concerned about whether the Americans would honour their commitments about investment should the takeover succeed. Concern is not just limited to the Coalition MPs, it would seem. And of course, Clive Palmer - who spoke out against the takeover this morning. Okedoky, while the Senate estimates continue, the lower house is preparing to meet at midday. We can expect more debate on the carbon tax repeal bills ahead of the vote expected on Thursday. In the meantime, Graham Readfearn reports that Australia has slipped down the climate change performance index as a result of trying to repeal the emission trading scheme. Australia has become an "anti-climate" influence on international efforts to slow global warming after dropping close to the bottom of a ranking of the world's largest greenhouse gas emitters. Australia's ranking on the climate change performance index fell from 51 to 57 out of 61, as the United Kingdom became the second highest ranked country behind Denmark. Our political editor Lenore Taylor: The Australian prime minister has refused to apologise for, or even talk about, the revelations of attempted spying against Indonesia revealed on Monday by Guardian Australia and the ABC, saying to do so was not in the interests of the bilateral relationship. Immigration officials are providing the nuanced arguments around the government's change in language from "irregular maritime arrivals" to "illegal maritime arrivals" when describing asylum seekers. Michael Pezzullo, the customs chief, at the Senate's Legal and Constitutional Affairs legislation committee, says "illegal" is a term that the department and government agencies took on after the election. We didn't generate it within the (public) service. Labor's Kim Carr: When you arrive by boat and seek asylum, how can you be illegal? Assistant immigration minister Michaelia Cash says the term "illegal refers specifically to that persons mode of arrival" without a proper visa, even though they do not commit an unlawful act when seeking asylum. Cash says turning up on our shores without a visa does breach the Migration Act and reminds estimates that many Labor ministers used the term "illegal". The problem appears to be that the term "unauthorised maritime arrival" is in the act. Labor senators want to know whether the government will create an offence for asylum seekers that will qualify the government to use the term "illegal". Martin Bowles, secretary of the immigration department, says he received the notification to use the term "illegal" in a letter from immigration minister Scott Morrison. Morning all, Katharine Murphy breaking in briefly on Gabi's live call of the day. While Ms Chan is monitoring simultaneously, Penny Wong's senate estimates mendacity crackdown, the increasing outrage of Jakarta and the world according to Clive Palmer .. and translating this in a flow of elegant prose .. I'm listening in currently on the customs service. Michael Pezzullo, the customs chief, is before the Senate's Legal and Constitutional Affairs legislation committee this morning. Pezzullo is updating senators on the efforts by customs management to root out corruption in the service. He's outlined the steps that have been taken to discipline or move on staff, the drug testing regime - and the current effort to crackdown on "hard to detect corruption and serious misconduct." Pezzullo says he's currently in discussion with the integrity commissioner about how to get to a bunch of folks he suggests are hiding, hoping to ride out recent scandals. He suggests those trying to wait it out should instead come forward. Pezzullo, who is known in Canberra for his straight talk, is also telling it like it is in terms of the customs budget. They are through the fat, through the muscle, and are in danger of breaching bone. "Our current operating model is not sustainble. This year the service will run at an operating loss for the scond year in a row," he says. "That's a very sad and frightening account you've just given," notes the Liberal senator Ian Macdonald. Photographer Lukas Coch caught the Indonesian ambassador before he flew back to Indonesia, under orders from his government. From Senate estimates, we discover that Australian public service natural attrition rates currently sit at 4.12 per cent, which suggests the numbers of public servants leaving and retiring add up to 26,069 over the next four years. Senator Wong believes the Coalition cannot achieve their 12,000 planned cuts completely through natural attrition and rejected suggestions that Labor had not disclosed their plans. Finance minister, Mathias Cormann: "Your cuts were not disclosed. Former finance minister, Penny Wong: "I will decline to respond to more mendacity. Endy Bayuni has written a comment piece for Guardian Australia regarding the spying allegations. He has translated comments made on Twitter by the Indonesian President SBY. Readers can find SBY's account on Twitter and then see his comments in Indonesian. Press "view translation" for an English version. Twitter's translation is slightly different in words to the translation below however the message remains the same. I regret the Australian PM statement belittling the phone-tapping in Indonesia without feeling guilty.We are reviewing a number of cooperation agenda because of the damaging Australian behaviour. Today, I have ordered FM Marty Natalegawa to recall the Indonesian ambassador to Australia. This is a decisive diplomatic step. The action by the US and Australia is damaging the strategic partnership with Indonesia, a democratic nation. One of SBY's tweet follows. Senate estimates continue with Penny Wong interrogating the department of finance officials and finance minister Mathais Cormann. Senator Wong is trying to nail down exactly how many public servants will go under the Coalition government given their stated plans to cut 12,000 jobs and minister Cormann is suggesting the former Labor government already had its own plans for widespread job cuts to the tune of 14,000. Cormann and Wong have a long sparring history, which is leaking into the hearings. Wong tells Cormann she is totally aware the Coalition won the election and gloating about it is not going to add value. My colleague Paul Farrell is reporting moves by the Australian Privacy Foundation in the wake of spying allegations. The Australian Privacy Foundation has urged the federal government to support a United Nations resolution bolstering international privacy protection in the wake of more revelations about Australia’s surveillance of Indonesian ministers. "As we find out what our national security agencies are doing overseas, it makes you wonder what they're doing within Australia,” the chairman of the foundation’s board, Roger Clarke, told Guardian Australia. Over at Fairfax, Philip Dorling has delivered a forthright message on Australia's efforts to bug the Indonesian leadership, Why do we do it? Behind all the declarations of friendship and good neighbourliness by successive Australian governments, Canberra just doesn't trust Jakarta. We work closely with Indonesia, including in the fields of security and intelligence, but we don't trust them. We never have, and probably never will. Greens Senator Scott Ludlum has called for an inquiry into Australia's electronic surveillance following the allegations that Australia spied on Indonesia. Ludlam says the spy agencies are not accountable to the Australian Parliament. Ludlum says the Australian Signals Directorate, allegedly responsible for the Indonesian surveillance in 2009, cannot be called before estimates committees, such as the ones held by the Senate this week. Ludlam says the public receives no annual report and we do not even know how many staff ASD employs. Ludlam lost a bid for a wide ranging Senate inquiry last week but in the light of the Indonesian revelations, he will have another crack at getting it up next week. Tony Abbott announced the government will provide $1 million to the White Ribbon campaign to help reduce violence against women, particularly in culturally and linguistically diverse communities. Clive Palmer has been haunting the courtyards of Parliament House to urge Treasurer Joe Hockey not to approve $3.4bn takeover by Archer Daniels Midland of Australian trader GrainCorp. He has called on the National Party, including deputy prime minister Warren Truss and agriculture minister Barnaby Joyce to sway their colleague against the decision, due on December 17. Good morning, The fallout from the Guardian/ABC revelations continues to spread across Australian politics like a creeping fog. The allegations that in 2009 under the Rudd government, Australian intelligence agencies were spying on the Indonesian President Susilo Bambang Yudhoyono and his wife Ani Yudhoyono were contained in documents leaked by NSA whistleblower, Edward Snowden. For a full round up over developments overnight, check out the report by Guardian correspondent Oliver Laugh land: Indonesia has recalled its ambassador to Australia and is reviewing all co-operation with the country after revelations that Australian spy agencies attempted to listen in to the phone calls of the Indonesian president and his inner circle. For the Indonesian perspective, check out Indonesia's top English language newspaper, The Jakarta Post: Foreign Minister Marty Natalegawa confirmed on Monday the government would recall Indonesian Ambassador to Australia Nadjib Riphat Kesoema following news reports suggesting that Australia had tried to listen to the phone calls of President Susilo Bambang Yudhoyono and his wife Ani Yudhoyono. Prime Minister Tony Abbott has been out early this morning, repeating his comments from Monday that Indonesia remains a great friend. I am not going to say anything or do anything to damage relations with our good friend Indonesia ... today may not be the best day in this relationship ... nevertheless we have a good relationship. While the Indonesian president has remained ominously silent, the Indonesian foreign minister Marty Natalegawa used a clear analogy for a sports loving nation like Australia. The ball is very much in Australia's court. Responding to Abbott's claims on Monday that all governments gather information on other governments, Natalegawa said "I've got news for you. We don't do it." Former foreign minister Bob Carr has been speaking to the ABC about the way forward, describing the public allegations as catastrophic and urged the Abbott government to apologise immediately. Carr refused to speculate on the specific allegations which occurred under Labor's watch. One of the more interesting perspectives this morning came from the Tasmanian independent member and former intelligence officer Andrew Wilkie who said it actually helps cement better relations for countries to know exactly what other leaders of thinking. He says when you don't know what they are thinking, it can make them worse. However he applauded Snowden's release of documents because "on balance it is in the public interest to know better what our agencies do." Wilkie says of what he knows of the business, he would be more surprised if Australia was not spying on Indonesia and "so much of what is going on in Indonesia is about the coming election".

results.webTitle: This payday loans decision gives me faith in politics | Serai Hann

fields.headline: This payday loans decision gives me faith in politics
fields.trailText: Serai Hann: I won't stop campaigning, but George Osborne's curbing of the cost of credit may mean others won't be harassed like I was
fields.bodyText: I was not interested in politics. I had never voted before. But earlier this year, I started campaigning with a group of friends in Swansea against the way we were being exploited by payday lenders. After taking a small loan to buy my kids Christmas presents, I ended up stuck in £1,000 of debt. And, being out of work, I was constantly worried about paying it back, with collectors ringing me daily for the money. Our campaign joined up with a national one, Sharkstoppers; a movement of communities organised by Movement for Change, fighting for fair credit in alliance with Labour MPs. Today, the chancellor, George Osborne, gave recognition to the campaign and the MPs, like Stella Creasy, who were behind it, announcing that the government will cap the interest rates that payday lenders can charge, and cap the total cost of credit. We don't have the details yet, but thsi decision has given me hope that my friends and people like us will be freed from the grip of these money lenders. And it's shown me that politics can work. This time last year, I had never bothered with politics. I didn't see the point. All it was to me was a bunch of people – mainly men – in suits, on TV, behaving worse than my two kids. But now I've seen it is much more than that. In Swansea, we brought together local people and institutions, like LGBT Unity, churches, trade unions, the Labour party and the credit union. We took action, got into negotiations and got our local council to act. We got our assembly members to act. We got the regional newspaper to give free coverage to fair credit and to consider banning high interest lenders from its pages. This month I went to London for the first time. There, I met people from Dundee to Southampton who had been running similar campaigns, and I saw just how powerful we are when we make politics work for us. On stage at the Labour party conference, my fellow activist Trina Burman and I asked the shadow minister for work and pensions, Rachel Reeves, to commit Labour to capping the cost of credit. When she did, we thanked her – and then Ed Miliband confirmed Labour would do it. The government has today responded as well, so it has our gratitude too. But we are going to keep campaigning. We want to end the way people are harassed by doorstep lenders. And we want to make sure people like me have access to fair credit, so that this Christmas I don't have to pay 5,000% interest just to get turkey on the table.

results.webTitle: Politics live: readers' edition - Friday 27 September

fields.headline: Politics live: readers' edition - Friday 27 September
fields.trailText: Share breaking news, leave links to interesting articles online and chat about the day's political events in our open thread
fields.bodyText: I'm not writing my usual Politics Live blog today, but, as an alternative, here's Politics Live: readers' edition. It's intended to be a place where you can catch up with the latest news and find links to good politics blogs and articles on the web. Please feel free to use this as somewhere you can comment on any of the day's political stories - just as you do when I'm writing the daily blog. It would be particularly useful for readers to flag up new material in the comments - breaking news or blogposts or tweets that are worth passing on because someone is going to find them interesting. A lot of what I do on my blog is aggregation - finding the good stuff and passing it on - and you can do this, too (as I know, because it happens every day when I'm blogging). All of today's Guardian politics stories are here, and all the politics stories filed on Thursday including some in today's paper, are here.

results.webTitle: Politics as it happened - Kevin Rudd's return - politics live blog

fields.headline: Politics as it happened - Kevin Rudd's return - politics live blog
fields.trailText: Kevin Rudd to be sworn in as prime minister, all the fallout from Labor's leadership change
fields.bodyText: The senate goes on sitting tomorrow. but as we've noted, the green chamber is up. The end of the 43rd parliament has been much like the parliament itself: exceptional, a bit wild, oversized, fabulous. It has been a privilege for me to cover this parliament - and I want to thank readers of politics live for such strong support since our launch in May. And hats of to Mike Bowers for exceptional work, always; but absolutely over the past few days. Let's say goodnight. Today opened with Tony Abbott signalling he would not subject Kevin Rudd to a no confidence motion. Kevin Rudd went to see the Governor-General and was sworn in as prime minister, alongside his deputy Anthony Albanese, and new Treasurer Chris Bowen. The last element of Julia Gillard's legislative legacy, the 457 visa legislation, passed the House of Representatives. MPs continued to bid politics farewell in emotional speeches. Abbott declared meanwhile the Coalition would hold Rudd to his record, not allow him to hit reset without a murmur. Fairfax Media's Jonathan Swan has filed a news update on the Slipper allegations and the latest responses. You can read that here. My colleague Lenore Taylor has just spoken again to Clive Palmer's spokesman on the issue of whether Joe Hockey or Mal Brough raised James Ashby's legal fees - would Palmer pay for Ashby's legal fees in a legal action taken against Slipper. (See posts just before and just after Question Time today for particulars.) Palmer's spokesman has switched course entirely from his previous advice to Lenore. He now says that the issue of payment of Ashby's legal fees was raised with Palmer by either Joe Hockey or Mal Brough - but he can't recall which person raised it. Hockey denied that he had raised the issue in a statement just after Question Time. For the history books. Julia Gillard takes leave of politics, and the chamber. That's it. Kevin Rudd exits the chamber and the 43rd parliament. Got to zip. Greens Senator Lee Rhiannon is speaking now in the Senate in the abortion debate. She's argues this is thin edge of the wedge stuff. She praises Julia Gillard for raising abortion rights in the final weeks of her prime ministership. This bill shows how right Ms Gillard was. This bill is a shot across the bows of Mr Abbott. She says it is yet to be seen whether Madigan would seek to use his influence with Tony Abbott post-election. She says this issue has flushed Abbott out. She notes Abbott's recent commitments not to change the abortion law. But she says his previous statements on women's reproductive rights are troubling. That's the end of the 43rd parliament in the House of Representatives. The House stands adjourned until it meets again for the 44th. That's it. Cash is the Coalition's spokesperson for the status of women. She argues gender selection abortion - a practice which occurs in China and in India - is a means of entrenching gender inequality. She says it occurs in countries where male children are prized above female children. She says there's no reliable evidence that gender selection abortions are occurring in this country. If it is happening, it is rare. Most abortions occur before the sex of the foetus is known. Cash refers to Abbott's recent commitment not to revisit the abortion law. She says it is a matter for the states predominantly. Now I apologise, I've had to chamber surf. I've tuned in now to the senate debate we foreshadowed earlier today on the Democratic Labor Party Senator John Madigan's bill to remove Medicare funding for gender selection abortions. Speaking presently is the Liberal Senator Michaelia Cash. I support Senator Madigan's right to stand up for his principles. Garrett closes on China. It's such an important relationship, but Australia needs to stand up for democratic values and human rights. And on growth versus sustainability. It can't be all about growth, it's got to be about the quality of growth. We have to protect the eco-system. Now to education reform. Garrett says he's grateful to Julia Gillard for the big task she gave him with Gonski. If we can put aisde the partisanship and the rancour, this package will deliver for kids, Garrett says. (Emerson - it is that kind of day.) Former Trade Minister Craig Emerson is sitting, listening, in a t-shirt. That kind of day. And shorts! When you come here you start from the ground up. I really wanted to learn the craft and apply myself to it. Garrett, on that transition. He came here with a great passion on the environment. What we've done on climate change will be regarded as a substantial transforming reform. He suggests the clean energy policy might be too modest, but it's rational and considered. He says the Greens haven't delivered much on conservation reform - the party that has done most for the environment is the Labor Party. I came into this place as a frontman and I became a team player. Peter Garrett reflects on the transformation from rock star to Cabinet minister. Stunts, he says, don't help you in the long term. Playing air guitar on the desk would dishonour his previous calling and his current one. You don't come here out of self interest, you come here out of public interest. Manager of Opposition Business Christopher Pyne, on Kevin Rudd's return. Mr Smug is back, writ large. Self indulgent, self regarding .. Pyne is on a Rudd roll. Pyne is asked to withdraw his remarks. I'm shocked but I withdraw. (He's shocked that self indulgent and self indulgent is unparliamentary.) The Liberals don't waste any time either. Here's the latest attack ad against Kevin Rudd. Colleagues reflect on his character. Kevin Rudd in his reply has challenged Abbott to some policy debates. Rudd says he's about building the house, not about knocking it down. Abbott suggests he's happy to have debates. Name the date, don't be scared, Abbott says, as Rudd exits the chamber. Tony Abbott is leading the matter of public importance debate this afternoon. Labor would like the public to move on from the extraordinary events of the past 24 hours - but the Coalition won't be moving on. The Coalition will be holding Rudd to account. He can't whitewash his record. Abbott would like to know when the election will be. Julia Gillard was brave enough to tell the public - why can't he? You should name the date. And what are your policies? Meanwhile, coming to an inbox near you, this salutation from the new Prime Minister. A reader has passed it on. The ALP are quick off the mark. Dear XXX In recent years, politics has failed the Australian people. There has just been too much negativity all round. There’s been an erosion of trust. Negative personal politics has done much to bring dishonour to our parliament but done nothing to address the urgent challenges facing our country, our communities and our families. In fact it has been holding our country back. This must stop, and with all my heart that is the purpose that I intend to pursue as Prime Minister. I want to acknowledge the achievements of my predecessor, Julia Gillard. She is a woman of extraordinary intelligence, of great strength and energy. She has achieved much under the difficult circumstances of minority government. Every effort I have in my being will be dedicated to uniting the Australian Labor Party. No retributions, no pay backs, none of that stuff. It is pointless, it is old politics. Together, we can win and defend all that Labor has achieved in the past six years. Join the campaign today or click here to donate. Thanks, Kevin Hockey is making a personal explanation concerning Peter Slipper's farewell speech just before Question Time. Slipper said of a meeting he had with mining magnate Clive Palmer earlier this year: I do recall that Mr (Clive) Palmer mentioned to me at that time that about Easter last year, Mr Brough, accompanied by the Member for North Sydney, came to see Mr Palmer to ask him to fund James Ashby's legal fees with respect to the litigation which people listening would be aware of. (We covered this statement from Slipper just before 2pm.) Mr Hockey did meet Palmer at that time. But he says Slipper's claims concerning Ashby and the legal fees are patently untrue. Hockey says he has never met Mr Ashby - and the discussion Slipper alluded to did not take place. He asks Slipper to correct the record. Slipper is now on his feet. He's attempting to repeat the allegation. But he's been sat down by Acting Speaker Bruce Scott. Slipper seeks indulgence. There is nothing to withdraw. I am simply repeating a conversation. Tony Abbott on Stephen Smith. Kevin Rudd. What do you say about Smithy? You are even more anally retentive than I am. Smith pays tribute to his core group of friends in politics. Anthony Albanese. A hero of the Labor movement in this parliament. Jenny Macklin, the Families Minister. Stephen Conroy, the Communications Minister. Conroy is misunderstood by many. (There's much laughter about that in the chamber.) Conroy is the bravest member of the parliament that I know. A man, he says, of raw courage, raw integrity. Wayne Swan. His closest friend. Swan, he says, put Labor in a position to win the election in 2007. He positioned Labor to take down John Howard on policy grounds, Smith says. Little bit gold, this picture. Rudd reacts to Stephen Smith's herogram for Stephen Conroy. (Rudd and Conroy are, let's put this politely, not very close.) Smith thanks Kevin Rudd and Julia Gillard for the opportunities he has been given in these two terms. I am very proud of what I have sought to do ... with regard to defence culture and the treatment of women. Smith says he will serve on the frontbench until September. But he's telling parliament on indulgence that he won't contest the election. It's time to retire. Twenty years I can do, twenty three years I can't. Rudd has placed further questions on the notice paper. Shadow Climate Action Minister Greg Hunt wants Kevin Rudd to account for deaths that occurred related to the controversial pink batts scheme - a government stimulus scheme that was poorly administered. Hunt wants to know if Rudd will table the warnings he got when he was previously prime minister about problems associated with the scheme. Rudd: Any industrial accident is one too many. Childcare Minister Kate Ellis is raising Judtih Sloan's recent remarks about the intelligence of childcare workers. (We covered the intervention by Professor Sloan - a columnist for The Australian, on the blog earlier this week. I believe she called childcare workers dim wits.) Minister Ellis is unhappy with Professor Sloan. On indulgence Tony Abbott makes his first remarks on this subject. I support moves to improve the professionalism of childcare centre workers. After all my wife runs a childcare centre. Tony Abbott lists Kevin Rudd's policy failures on boat arrivals. Kevin Rudd says Abbott's tow back policy will not work. Rudd is quoting the Indonesian Amabassador's position in late May - he says the Indonesians won't do what Abbott suggests they will do. Australian defence officials have said tow back doesn't work. Rudd: If we are having a debate in this country it should be about policies that work rather than soundbites which sound good. Rudd says he's seeking security briefings on the latest concerning boat arrivals. He suggests Abbott should do the same. Or is he frightened of facing the facts? Shadow treasurer Joe Hockey wants to know if Kevin Rudd will scrap the carbon tax and the increase due next Monday. Rudd: I have long been committed to a carbon price. Those opposite have a little to explain on this question. Rudd would like to know why the Opposition has departed from its previous support for emissions trading under the Howard Government. He points to global action on climate change. Kevin Rudd is telling the shadow treasurer Joe Hockey that Cabinet will meet next week, most likely. And he'll be seeking briefings from Treasury. Tony Abbot is on Kevin Rudd's broken promises. Kevin Rudd is on message - sorry, on policy. Why don't you debate me about policy Tony? Rudd: All we get here is politics politics politics. My colleague Lenore Taylor has checked Peter Slipper's claim just before Question Time that Mal Brough and Joe Hockey approached Clive Palmer concerning the payment of James Ashby's legal fees. Lenore has spoken to Mr Palmer's spokesman. This is his quote: Clive has denied this about a thousand times. One more quick back track. Here's Mike Bowers beautiful frame of Julia Gillard's reaction to Rob Oakeshott's valedictory speech. Your father would have been proud of you. Superb stuff. And his lovely version for Twitter. The new backbench. I need to back track briefly. There was that Slipper squeeze just before Question Time, apologies. Rudd in his first answer has thrown the election date up in the air. He's said he will revert to the practice of previous prime ministers - meaning he doesn't intend to telegraph the date in advance. Ah no. Deputy Liberal leader Julie Bishop inquires why the Australian people can trust Rudd when he promised not to challenge Julia Gillard for the Labor leadership. Rudd says he's explained why he changed his mind. Bishop is back with a supplementary. Why didn't you keep your word to the Member for Lalor? I refer her to my statement from yesterday. I have nothing further to add. Rudd: It's time for the old politics of negativity to be dead and buried. Having observed from the nether regions these past few years .. Prime Minister Kevin Rudd, back at the dispatch box, for his first Question Time. I am seeking comment from Joe Hockey's spokesman concerning Peter Slipper's allegation in that valedictory. Slipper says he signed up the former Howard Government minister and LNP candidate, Mal Brough. We helped him. We signed him up. In a sense, I am responsible for my own predicament. Slipper says the mining magnate Clive Palmer invited him recently to breakfast at his resort. I think it was held in the Titanic room. A very large breakfast for two people and we had a very interesting conversation. I do recall that Mr Palmer mentioned to me at that time that about Easter last year, Mr Brough, accompanied by the Member for North Sydney, came to see Mr Palmer to ask him to fund James Ashby's legal fees with respect to the litigation which people listening would be aware of. The former Attorney-General, Ms Roxon, the Member for Gellibrand, mentioned the possibility of an Ashbygate royal commission. I do understand that matter is under active consideration. I would hope the government moves to see the involvement of members of the oppositon in particular in what has resulted in a situation where there was an attempt to not only remove shall we say the speaker of the parliament, but also bring down the government of this nation. Ah, the meaning of this intervention is becoming clear. This is a speech about James Ashby. This is somewhat unusual. Former Speaker Peter Slipper is giving a farewell speech just in case he decides not to run, or in case he loses. Oakeshott tells the chamber about a text message he sent Gillard last night, He said your father would be proud of you. Gillard is struggling to keep her emotions in check. It is a very profound moment, the respect that has passed between these two people. Oakeshott says some truly disgusting things have been said over this past three years. We've got to deal with ugly Australia. We've got to deal with it somehow. I adore this picture from Mike Bowers. Suspect my blog format doesn't do it justice, but it's full of atmosphere. Julia Gillard enters the chamber for the first time as a backbencher to listen to Rob Oakeshott make his farewell. On his infamous 17 minute statement before backing Julia Gillard and Labor in 2010 - Oakeshott makes no apology. I own that 17 minutes for the work contained within it. He says everything he said in that 17 minutes has been delivered. Joke away world if you like. (The world does joke away on the 17 minutes, it's true). On the first minority parliament in Canberra since World War Two. I think it has been an enriching experience to be part of it. I hope the convenient rhetoric doesn't kill the reality. Oakshott says more than 80 per cent of the legislation in this parliament has been bipartisan. Confidence has been maintained. Three budgets passed. I leave optimistic and I leave confident. Parliament and democracy is bigger than its intrigues. Thank you prime minister. Oakeshott pauses, horrified at his faux pas. Julia Gillard has entered the chamber to listen. Thank you Julia. Crossbencher Rob Oakeshott has begun his farewell speech. The moment's right for me. I'm basically a bit tired. There we go. The last bit of Julia GIllard's legislative legacy is through the lower house. This is a prime minister who has failed the first test of decency. Scott Morrison, on the 457 bill. Kevin Rudd has rolled over to the unions is his argument. The House is now dividing for the third reading. This bill is just about to pass the House of Representatives. Once were ministers. Team Gillard. Here comes the vote on the 457 legislation. The procedural skirmishes are continuing. The government is attempting to push through on 457 visas. Scott Morrison is trying to head that off. He's attempting to bring on a valedictory speech from Rob Oakeshott. The Speaker Anna Burke is unimpressed. I'm loath to use a Member's valedictory in this way. The Speaker guides the House back to 457 visas. The Opposition is objecting. Another division is underway. Deputy Prime Minister Albo. Welcome back Kevin. The sequel. Welcome back Kevin. Firmly into the procedural fray now. The government is attempting to bring the 457 legislation back on. Shadow Immigration Minister Scott Morrison is objecting. At some length. Ah yes, here's the attack line. The new prime minister started the boats. He can't be trusted to stop the boats. Morrison says the 457 visa legislation is a smoke screen by the government to distract the public from its failures on border protection. And who was responsible for the failures? One KRudd (as one KRudd is inclined to say). The Opposition leader congratulates the prime minister on achieving his ambition to return to The Lodge. Tony Abbott: May he elevate that office, and he would best elevate that office by explaining the events of last night. Abbott adds that Rudd would also elevate the office by telling the Australian voters when they will get an election. Abbott makes the reflection that politics is sometimes far more brutal than it needs to be. There is some murmuring at that observation. Rudd shook hands with Tony Abbott as he entered the chamber for the first time as prime minister .. well of course .. the first time as prime minister since 2010. Rudd informs the House there has been a leadership change. He's been commissioned as the prime minister. I would like to acknowledge the achievements of the former prime minister and the former treasurer. Rudd repeats his remarks about Julia Gillard's positive policy legacy from last night - and he acknowledges her as a standard bearer for women. Australia's first female prime minister. Swan gets a positive wrap for his work during the global financial crisis. Rudd informs the House that Anthony Albanese will take questions during question time on behalf of the ministers who exited their posts overnight. There will be announcements to follow. Rudd says politics is a hard game. Let us try, try .. and be a little kinder and gentler to each other in the deliberations of this parliament. Shadow Treasurer Joe Hockey has just shaken hands with Chris Bowen down in the chamber. Let the new joust begin. I think we'll take stock before the tempo of the political day accelerates at noon. The Canberra day thus far, let's keep this simple: Tony Abbott is signalling he doesn't intend to move a no-confidence motion in the wake of the Labor leadership change. Kevin Rudd is back in the The Lodge, he was sworn in at Government House this morning with his new deputy, Anthony Albanese, and his new Treasurer, Chris Bowen. Current advice suggests the 457 legislation will be debated later on. There will be a senate debate this afternoon about John Madigan's private member's bill on gender selection abortions. Question Time - how Rudd will manage that in a logistical sense given a bunch of frontbenchers have stood down and are yet to be replaced - is a bit of a mystery to me. We shall see. Rudd statement to parliament coming right up. Greens deputy leader Adam Bandt is already in campaign mode in the Mural Hall of parliament house. The Greens are stable, united and progressive. Unlike .. Well, you can fill in the blanks. David Marr brings his wonderful facility to the subject of Kevin Rudd. You can read the full piece here. Here's a taste. The plotters who pulled Kevin Rudd down in 2010 didn’t know their man. They knew he was making a mess of government and had lost support in the caucus. They could see his immense popularity fading. But they didn’t realise that Rudd would never give up, the wounds would never heal and he would never go away. His self-belief is bottomless. There were dark nights for Rudd after his defenestration but it remained a constant comfort that he had never been rejected by the Australian people. His quarrel was with his party, a quarrel he was ready to resume whenever the opportunity allowed. 457s - Rudd's office guidance evidently remains as it was to me earlier. This legislation will be considered today. That's the plan. Thanks to Fairfax Media's Daniel Hurst. A lot going on today around the place. The Senate will this afternoon reflect on the Democratic Labor Senator John Madigan's private member's bill which seeks to remove Medicare funding for "gender selection" abortions. The debate will take place in private member's time, which is after 4.30pm. At this stage, the debate has three speakers. Here's Guardian Australia political editor Lenore Taylor's news lead. The day thus far. Kevin Rudd has been sworn in as prime minister for a second time, three years after being deposed, and now faces the daunting task of reworking policy and replacing more than one-third of the cabinet just months before an election. Rudd has not said when he will call the poll or whether he will stick with Julia Gillard’s nominated election date of 14 September, but appears to have the confidence of the hung parliament. The Coalition is indicating it will not move a no confidence motion. As the nation woke, yet again, to find the prime minister had been changed overnight and without reference to the voters, Labor struggled to work through the divisions that resulted in the overthrow of Australia’s first female prime minister in a ballot won by Rudd, 57 votes to 45. Rudd backer Chris Bowen who resigned from the cabinet after the last leadership crisis in March, has been confirmed as treasurer. Gillard backer Gary Gray said he had agreed to stay on as resources minister, but was still blunt in his assessment that Gillard’s leadership had been killed off by a constant campaign of destabilisation by Rudd supporters. Gray only last week said Rudd was able to “get himself into the media … what he can’t do is govern and what he can’t do is lead the Labor party”. He has told ABC radio he made the remarks “in the heat of anger and frustration”. Speaking after the ballot on Wednesday night, a composed Gillard said she was proud of her achievements in what “has not been an easy environment to work in” because of the minority parliament, the internal Labor divisions and the Coalition’s fierce anti-carbon tax campaign. I've already referenced Penny Wong's interview this morning on Radio National Breakfast. It was compelling listening. Here's an excerpt - Wong on Gillard and the gender question. FRAN KELLY: Julia Gillard was Australia’s first female Prime Minister. How hard was it for you to vote against Australia’s first female Prime Minister? Here you are, a senior woman in a Labor Cabinet. PENNY WONG: And a feminist. And that’s been extremely hard. But, ultimately, as I said, you have to weigh your loyalties and your different principles. And I do not believe the feminist principles which I hold dear would be served by Tony Abbott becoming Prime Minister with the views that he has expressed on women and women’s capacity and because of the ways in which he has implicitly allowed the sexism in this country that we have seen on display. I do not believe a leopard changes his spots. FRAN KELLY: In terms of the sexism which has been on display, as you say, the Prime Minister made some reference to that last night too – the former Prime Minister, Julia Gillard, in her speech after the vote. She said: “The reaction to being the first woman Prime Minister does not explain everything about my Prime Ministership, nor does it explain nothing. It explains some things and it’s for the nation to think about those things in a sophisticated way”. Do you think the reaction to having a female Prime Minister ... how much of it does explain about Julia Gillard’s position in the polls with the electorate? PENNY WONG: I think Julia put it very well – “it explains some things, but not everything”. And I think her suggestion that we collectively consider as a nation the response and, in particular, the way in which things were said, the sorts of views and personal attacks that became legitimised, we should think about whether that is what we want for our national politics. As Prime Minister Rudd said last night, that is not what we want for our national politics. We should have a very different approach to our political life and I hope that we do consider that. FRAN KELLY: Julia Gillard also said last night that she thinks her doing this job will make it easier for the next woman. You could argue it could make it harder, given the reaction? PENNY WONG: Well, it depends what we do with it. I think she’s right. The important thing about being the first is that there’s another, and then another. There's fifty of his closest friends waiting outside the ABC studios in parliament house. Workplace Minister Bill Shorten is doing a round of interviews after his decision to back Kevin Rudd in last night's leadership ballot. He's being asked, in different constructions, how it feels to be a traitor. Shorten's playing a dead bat on that. Kevin Rudd gives Labor its best chance of saving its policy legacy, Shorten says. Bit of confusion around about the fate of the 457 visa legislation - the proposal Julia Gillard championed to limit employer's use of temporary skilled migration. It's not on the list for consideration by parliament today. There's some suggestions around that the legislation has been pulled - but Kevin Rudd's people are telling me it is meant to be on the list. Clear as mud. When I know one way or the other, I will tell you. Here's the official photo. The Rudds are back in The Lodge. Ms Josephine. And her parents - Albert Tse and Jessica Rudd. This is the current guidance: We expect Rudd to make a statement to parliament at noon today. That will be followed by Question Time. We expect Rudd to unveil the remainder of his ministry tomorrow. I will do my very best. That's what Rudd just told the Governor-General Quentin Bryce. Aussies update. Julia Gillard left the cafe just as Kevin Rudd exchanged the paperwork to become prime minister. That's just happened by the way. It's offical. It's Prime Minister Rudd. Now it's Deputy Prime MInister Albanese. He's just been sworn in. Baby Josephine is burbling away contentedly. Underway now at Government House. Rudd is looking very attentive, bolt upright in his seat, clutching his bible. Now down at Aussies Cafe in parliament house, a different scene. Julia Gillard and Wayne Swan are having a coffee. Swan is wearing a hoodie. They are sitting at a table in front of a television which is broadcasting today's swearing in ceremony. My spies at Aussie Cafe tell me they are having a laugh and a chat with staff - and ignoring the pictures. A bible for Kevin. Anthony Albanese's wife Carmel Tebbutt - a former deputy Labor leader herself - is at Government House with her husband. Mike Bowers on the spot. The Rudd family have just pulled up at Government House. Therese Rein. Daughter Jessica. Grand-daughter Josephine. All smiles. I'm absolutely confident that Kevin Rudd will be making some key policy announcements over the coming days. That's Rudd supporter and Labor backbencher Stephen Jones, on Sky News. Policy, policy, policy. Here comes Kevin Rudd, up Dunrossil Drive. I do love that avenue. Just recapping in case you watched the football last night. Julia Gillard's farewell press conference. Greens leader Christine Milne, flanked by her deputy Adam Bandt, is in the courtyard telling reporters she has informed Kevin Rudd he has confidence provided there is an election by the end of September. Bandt is reflecting on Gillard's exit. I don't think her own side ever gave her a fair go. There's been years of destabilisation and back stabbing. The ultimate winner out of that has been Tony Abbott. Nick Champion, Labor backbencher, is on Sky News arguing there is a deep reservoir of affection for Kevin Rudd out there in voter-land. Moving forward. Order of the day. Who says there's no gratitude in politics? Penny for your thoughts Tony. Abbott on the lawns for the breakfast TV parade this morning. The swearing in will also include Anthony Albanese as Rudd's deputy - and Chris Bowen, who returns to the frontbench as Treasurer. Bowen is a strong performer who has been wasted on the backbench courtesy of Labor's civil war. It will be good to see him step up again. It appears from what the crossbenchers have been saying this morning that Rudd would have had the numbers on the floor of the parliament to survive any confidence test. The legal advice underpinning Kevin Rudd's swearing in as prime minister has also been released this morning. If you want to have a look at the relevant correspondence, look here. We expect Rudd to be sworn in at 9.30am this morning. One of the big questions hanging over any transition by Labor back to Kevin Rudd as leader and prime minister has been whether the new government would survive a no-confidence motion. Rather a big deal, that issue. But Tony Abbott this morning is clearly walking back from the idea that he would move a motion of no confidence in Rudd. Here's Abbott on breakfast television: KARL STEFANOVIC: As you were walking over, I was saying that you seem to be moving away from the no confidence motion. Is that the case this morning? TONY ABBOTT: Look, plainly one-third of the Cabinet has no confidence in Mr Rudd. They have resigned. It’s the biggest exodus from the Cabinet in memory. But we are not into parliamentary games. There have been far too many parliamentary games, there’s been far too much backroom dealing and double crossing. Let's let the people decide. That’s what we need. KARL STEFANOVIC: You’ve talked about that, though, in the past. You’ve obviously changed your mind on that. Why have you have changed your mind on the no confidence motion? TONY ABBOTT: Well, I’ve always said it’s the people's responsibility to choose the Prime Minister and to choose the government. All we have had for the last three years are backroom deals and back-stabbing orchestrated by the faceless men who are not interested in the welfare of the Australian people, not interested in the all the battlers out there who are struggling with the cost of living pressures, who wonder why our borders are in chaos. They’re just interested in protecting their own power and prerogatives. An air of exhaustion hangs over the place today - but that hasn't stopped the new guard hitting the airwaves this morning. Penny Wong and Mark Butler - two prominent ministers and two key players in Labor's transition back to Rudd - have confirmed this morning that they voted for Rudd in yesterday's ballot. Wong obviously faced a very difficult personal decision. She told Fran Kelly on Radio National breakfast it was a testing proposition for a feminist - walking away from the country's first female prime minister. But Wong said her feminist principles were best served ultimately by ensuring Labor had the best strategy to keep Tony Abbott from winning the election. Good morning and welcome to the day after the night before. Mike Bowers has been out early documenting the transition to a new prime minister. He's captured a handshake between two of Kevin Rudd's great supporters, lobbyist and strategist Bruce Hawker and Senator Doug Cameron. It's like a circus down on the front lawns of the parliament - all the TV networks have set up their breakfast shows to broadcast from Canberra. Our two protagonists met up after some punditry on morning TV. I won’t bombard you all with an exhaustive list of stories about the leadership in our news wrap this morning - I’m going to assume, given the readership yesterday, that you are across what happened, more or less. But I will point you in the direction of a few pieces by my favourite writers on a completely extraordinary day. Michael Gordon: The Age Julia Gillard has delivered the ultimate act of leadership and paid the ultimate price, ending the most poisonous, inglorious chapter in modern Labor Party history. But whether Kevin Rudd transforms Labor's prospects at the election depends on whether he can re-unite a fractured party that he helped divide, re-connect with a hostile electorate and challenge a rampant opposition. Gillard's magnanimity in the end has made the transition easier; her decisiveness ensured that this was the last stand. Yes, her flaws made her vulnerable, but she will be remembered as one of the nation's most tenacious leaders. Paul Kelly: The Australian (paywalled) Kevin Rudd is resurrected, Julia Gillard's career is destroyed and Rudd has launched a new crusade to halt the Tony Abbott bandwagon. This is the dramatic trifecta that pivots the 2013 election upon an unpredictable new axis. In grim desperation the Labor Party has voted for its self-survival in a bid to deny or cripple an Abbott government. Lenore Taylor: Guardian Australia The future of the party now hinges on whether voters still like Rudd when he is leader again, or whether their professed preference for him was really a hankering for the way they felt when they elected him as "Kevin07", and politics still felt hopeful and positive – in contrast to the down and dirty shouting match it has been for the past three years. Laura Tingle: The Australian Financial Review (paywalled) Kevin Rudd finally returns to a prime ministership diminished by the Labor civil war that first removed him, helped create minority government and then replaced him. And in which he, of course, was a major protagonist. His will to power, and all the flaws it has exposed along the way, now turn on Tony Abbott. His goal will be an election victory – not saving the furniture – though the timing of that election must now be in doubt. Michelle Grattan: The Conversation Rudd has been restored to the leadership three years to the week after he was pushed out. In his mind, his return journey has all been about righting a wrong, seizing back what was his – the power, the Prime Ministerial Office, the Lodge. This rang through his news conference tonight, when he said: “In 2007 the Australian people elected me to be their PM. That is the task that I resume today …” Rudd’s tortuous course back has been costly to the party and contributed to, although is not responsible for, Gillard’s failures. We'll be following all today's events live. You can join in the conversation on Twitter by using the hashtag #politicslive or follow me there @murpharoo

results.webTitle: Politics Weekly podcast: Labour party conference 2013

fields.headline: Politics Weekly podcast: Labour party conference 2013
fields.trailText: Michael White, Heather Stewart and Mark Ferguson join Hugh Muir to discuss Ed Miliband's various pledges
fields.bodyText: Ed Miliband promised to stand up for consumers against the big six energy companies with a pledge to freeze gas and electricity prices for the 20 months following the next election. Labour says the move would save the average household £120 and forms a major part of the party's vow to help with what it calls a "cost of living crisis". In a speech delivered without notes, Miliband also pledged to support voting rights for 16- and 17-year-olds and build 200,000 new homes. Joining Hugh Muir this week to discuss it all are the Guardian's columnist Michael White; the Observer's economics editor Heather Stewart and the editor of Labour List Mark Ferguson. Also this week: we pick through the revelations in Damian McBride's book on his time at the heart of Gordon Brown's communications team. And we look ahead to David Cameron's challenge at his party's conference in Manchester next week. Please leave your thoughts below.

results.webTitle: Prescott, Blair, Straw – the next generation of UK politics?

fields.headline: Prescott, Blair, Straw – the next generation of UK politics?
fields.trailText: David Prescott is one of many with familiar surnames hoping to make it into parliament. Emine Saner looks at our Westminster dynasties and what they say about politics today
fields.bodyText: David Prescott was born just a few days after his father John became MP for Hull East. "So I've known nothing else," he says. Before he was four years old, he was on the campaign trail in the 1974 general election – "I used to go round in the car saying 'vote Yaybour'; I couldn't pronounce the letter L" – and he has volunteered in every election since 1983. Politics was in the family. "Labour activism didn't start with John," he says. "My grandmother was a member of Jack Jones's pensioners' parliament [the National Pensioners' Convention] and going back, there were politically active miners as well. If you're the son or daughter of an MP, people think it's just because of your father or mother. But actually it goes much further back and you find activism goes through a whole family." Prescott is standing for selection to be Labour's prospective parliamentary candidate (PPC) for south London's Greenwich and Woolwich seat tomorrow, after its current MP, Labour's Nick Raynsford, announced he would stand down at the next election. It's the third time he has sought selection – in 2008, he stood for his father's Hull East seat, two years later he sought to become Labour's PPC in Weaver Vale, Runcorn. Now he's trying again in the constituency where he has lived for the past 13 years. "I've never known a selection process that's had such a strong list of candidates," he says by way of countering any accusation he has been parachuted in. "I don't look round and find where my best chance of trying to get in is." Did he grow up wanting to become an MP? "Not really. I've always been active, but I made a very clear decision that I wanted to have a life outside politics." Prescott became a journalist, and now runs a PR company. "When Nick announced he was standing down, I had a long chat with my wife about it because I know the impact it can have on families." He describes rarely seeing his father, who would return home on Thursday and then spend the next couple of days on constituency business before heading back to Westminster. But it was an early life filled with political giants – Barbara Castle and the trade unionist Rodney Bickerstaffe were family friends. When he moved to London, he moved into a flat with his father and Dennis Skinner. "So when you grow up in that environment you can see the good that politics can do, on a very local level, how it can change people's lives. So it's hard not to think that would be a decent and honourable thing to do. I've always made it clear in my own mind that if the time was right, I'd find my own way." If Prescott were to become Labour's PPC – and Greenwich and Woolwich is a safe Labour seat – he will join a political class where family ties are not exactly rare. It almost feels as if it could take less time to run through the politicians, or potential politicians, who aren't related. There are siblings – Keith and Valerie Vaz, Maria and Angela Eagle, Boris and Jo Johnson (whose father Stanley was an MEP, and in a reversal to the usual pattern, attempted to succeed his son in Henley when Boris stepped down). Until recently, there were the Milibands. And there are the couples – Ed Balls and Yvette Cooper, Harriet Harman and Jack Dromey, Jo Swinson and Duncan Hames. Then there are the numerous children of politicians. The Conservatives have Francis Maude (son of Angus). Bernard Jenkin (whose father Charles, now Lord Jenkin, was an MP for more than 20 years), Nick Hurd (son of Douglas), Ben Gummer (son of John) and Andrew Mitchell (son of David). Dominic Grieve, the attorney general, is the son of Percy Grieve, who was a Conservative MP for nearly 20 years. How far back should we trace Nicholas Soames's political lineage? He is the greatgrandson of chancellor Randolph Churchill, and grandson of Winston Churchill; both his father, Christopher Soames, and uncle, Duncan Sandys, were members of parliament. Labour looks just as cosy. The MSP Paul Martin is the son of former speaker Michael Martin. Anas Sarwar took his father's vacated Glasgow Central seat in 2010. Andy Sawford, recently elected MP for Corby, is the son of Phil Sawford, who had the neighbouring Kettering constituency until 2005. And those are just the current MPs. There are also those who tried and failed to win seats, such as Tamsin Dunwoody, who contested her mother's seat in 2008.
