That was the problem. He had not had media training, he says. But Jones and MacKaill had had media training. James Clappison goes next. He says Mitchell's attitude at the meeting was "contrite". Chris Ruane, the Labour MP, goes next. Q: Do you think Mitchell was right to resign? Yes, says MacKaill. Q: Don't you feel any sympathy for him? Don't you think you owe him an apology? MacKaill says Mitchell had dismissed police integrity. At the time MacKaill did not know what subsequently emerged about the Number 10 incident. Vaz goes next. Q: Did you know that the National Federation had stopped using John Gaunt because he had stopped following the instructions of his client? Hinton says he did not know that. Q: You had a PR person with you. He drove to the meeting with you. He was receiving calls from the media. And he told you to finish the meeting before the 6pm news. You were acting with a view to discredit a senior minister. MacKaill says he refutes that. Q: When you spoke to BBC Midlands you were not telling the truth. Hinton says he does not accept that. Q: You said Mitchell spoke with candour. Then you said he had not given a full account. Hinton says the reference to "candour" was a reference to the way Mitchell made it clear he had not used the word "pleb". Q: How did the media find out about the location of the meeting? Mitchell would not have told them. Hinton says the Police Federation's PR adviser, John Gaunt, was under orders not to tell the media about the location of the meeting. Vaz says Gaunt will be called to give evidence. Michael Ellis says Hinton's use of the phrase "that woman" was a clear reference to Theresa May. It is disgraceful. Hinton should apologise, he says. Keith Vaz goes next. He quotes from the transcript. Hinton said the Police Federation had problems with "that woman" from the Conservatives. Who was that? Hinton says he does not recall using the phrase "that woman". He thinks there is a mistake in the transcript. Labour's David Winnick goes next. Jones says the campaign against cuts was a legitimate one. Q: Haven't your actions undermined the campaign against the cuts? Jones says things have been taken out of context. If they were to run the campaign again, they would do it in a different way. At the time his members were very supportive, Jones says. Mark Reckless, the Conservative MP, goes next. Hinton says he was subject to the investigation, but not involved in it. Q: Were you there on Police Federation time? Yes, says Hinton. He says full-time federation officials are paid by the police to carry out union duties. Q: Should taxpayers' money be spent on this? Hinton says they should be able to represent their members. Police officers do not have normal employment rights, he says. Steve McCable, the Labour MP, goes next. He says the "plebgate" affair has distracted attention from the campaign against cuts. Keith Vaz is asking the questions now. Q: This was about the cuts. Did you get a letter from the chairman of the national Police Federation (the late Paul McKeever) asking you not to carry on with your campaign. MacKaill said he did get a letter. But the Police Federation had no power to tell them to stop. Q: The national federation was concerned about the personal nature of your campaign. MacKaill says they had the support of their local members, and the public. Vaz says the meeting ended at 5.45pm, even though they had not yet discussed the cuts. He suggests that was because he wanted to get on the evening news. Hinton says he was no there to conduct an interview with Mitchell. He was there to clear the air. He says that when Mitchell said he did not use the words "pleb" or "moron", that was the first time he had been that specific. Clappison says the officers said that Mitchell did not tell them what he said. But he had done. James Clappison, the Conservative, goes next. Q: You said Andrew Mitchell did not tell you want he said. But he did. MacKaill says his comment has been taken out of context. He reads a full version of what he said. MacKail goes on: He gave an account but he did not tell us exactly what he said. Q: Do you think it is in your interests for this to be redetermined? Jones says the chief constables have reviewed this. Q: So you think all is fine now? Hinton says they submitted themselves to an inquiry. Q: But the chief constable now wants this reopened. Hinton says he did not know that. MacKaill says he would be surprised by that. He would like to know the reasons. Jones says the police should follow due process. He thinks due process has been followed. Jones has served 28 years in the police, MacKaill 22 years, and Hinton 21 years. Q: Do you know that the report said you gave a misleading account? Jones says he knows that now. The three Police Federation officers are here. They are: Sergeant Chris Jones from West Midlands Police Federation, Detective Sergeant Stuart Hinton from Warwickshire Police Federation and Inspector Ken MacKaill from West Mercia Police Federation. Keith Vaz starts. Q: You issues an apology. It seems very half-hearted. Is it an apology to Mitchell. Jones says the officers did represent that meeting correctly. Q: So there is no apology to Mitchell? Jones says is an apology for the choreography. Q: You think you have done nothing wrong. That's right, says Jones. MacKaill says he gave what he thought was an accurate account to the media. • Deborah Glass, the IPCC deputy chair, has said that she thought the three officers should have been found guilty of gross misconduct. She said she was surprised when the final report dropped the conclusion that they were guilty of misconduct and instead said there was no case to answer. • She said that she would consider whether there were legal grounds for the IPCC to reopen the inquiry. Keith Vaz is asking questions now. Q: Is it a good diea for police officers to carry recording cameras, as David Davis recommends? Owers says she would not like to extrapolate from one case. But she would point out that when the PACE said interviews should be recorded the police objected. Yet now it is recognised as as good idea. Steve McCabe, the Labour MP, goes next. Q: Would you says it is "almost impossible" to understand how the police arrived at this decision. Glass says the evidence and the conclusion are so at odd she felt the need to issue a statement. Q: Do you think Mitchell deserves an apology? Glass says she has focused on the misconduct. Q: Were you surprised how the police reacted to the Glass statement last week. Some officers said the IPCC should be abolished? Owers says she did not hear that. Owers says the IPCC can only supervise certain investigations because of its limited resources. That is unsatisfactory, she says. Julian Huppert, the Lib Dem MP, goes next. Q: Why did the IPCC go for a supervised investigation (ie, letting the police investigate this themselves, with supervision from the IPCC), instead of doing it themselves? Glass says the IPCC has to live in the real world. It has limited resources. Unlike the police, it cannot just borrow officers from forces. I would have loved to have done it. But it just was not practical. Mark Reckless, a Conservative, goes next. Q: The final report says the IO [investigation officer] thought there was no case to answer. Who did you take that to be? Glass says she would have taken that to refer to Jerry Reakes-Williams. She says the authorities would have been entitled to ignore his recommendation. The conclusions were "extraordinary", she says. Q: Why did the IPCC take over the investigation? Glass says that to do that just to rewrite the conclusions would have been an abuse of process. She says if there is a way of reopening it lawfully, she would. Q: If the chief constables say to us they would like the IPCC to now deal with this, you will take legal advice to see if you can do this? Glass says she will look into this. The chief constables themselves definitely can reopen this investigation, she says. Nicola Blackwood, a Conservative, goes next. Glass says nothing gave her concern until she saw the final report on 28 August recommending no further action. Michael Ellis, a Conservative, goes next. Q: The investigating officer came to a conclusion. But he was usurped. Is this a whitewash? Glass says we are certainly dealing with confusion. Q: But do you feel that this is an attempt at a whitewash? In your statement last week you said the Police Federation were running a political campaign. Glass says she thinks the conclusions were wrong. Chris Ruane, the Labour MP, goes next. Q: Mitchell had a 45-second conversation. This has now gone on for a year. Is that too long? Glass says the IPCC did not deal with the Number 10 incident. That was investigated by the Metropolitan Police. Owers says the IPCC has limited reources. Owers says the appropriate authorities knew what Glass's view was. James Clappison, a Conservative, goes next. He quotes from a letter from Andy Parker, the Warwickshire chief constable, saying there were irregularities with the investigation. Glass says she was not aware of that at the time. Asked if she thought Andrew Mitchell gave a full account of what happened to the officers, she says he answered all the questions he was asked. The point about not giving a full account sounds like an ex post facto argument, she says. Dame Anne Owers, the IPCC chair, and Deborah Glass, the IPCC deputy chair, are giving evidence now. Glass says she thinks the report should have found the three guilty of gross misconduct. Q: Do you think they lied? Glass reads from the statement she put out last week. A misconduct should have determined that, she says. Q: You have been doing this for 13 years. Is there any precedent for a draft report saying misconduct, your advising gross misconduct, and then the final report being even weaker. Glass says it is not unusual for reports to change from draft to final version. But in this case she could not see how the conclusion was justified. Q: Do you think the IPCC should have carried out the investigation itself? Glass says she had no problem with the way the inquiry was carried out. It was just the conclusion she could not accept. She took legal advice on what she could do. That led to her putting out her statement. Here is a snap summary, • Keith Vaz has said that David Shaw, the West Mercia chief constable, has now changed his mind about whether the three officers should face disciplinary action. • Jerry Reakes-Williams, the Professional Standards officer who investigated the three officers, has confirmed that he originally concluded the officers were guilty of misconduct, but that he was overruled. • Reakes-Williams has said that he did not think the officers deliberately lied about their meeting with Andrew Mitchell. We are now on to the IPCC witnesses. Keith Vaz says RW has been very open. But the public will conclude you thought there was misconduct, you had a meeting with two deputy chief constables, and then that recommendation was removed. He says the chief constable of West Mercia has submitted evidence to the committee saying there was evidence of misconduct. It backs up what RW said. That will be published by the committee at 5.30, Vaz says. RW says no one put pressure on him to change his stance. Labour's Steve McCabe goes next. Q: How would you rate yourself as an investigator? RW says there may have been procedural issues. But at the time no one picked these up at the time, including the IPCC. Mark Reckless, a Conservative, goes next. Q: The IPCC told you not to share your conclusions with the authorities (representatives of the chief constable). But you did . Why? RW says he thought it was important that they were aware of the difference of opinion. Q: But you were the investigating officer? RW says it was clear he was the investigation officer. But in police terms his deputy may have been seen as the investigating officer. He says the IPCC told him to forward a copy of his report without the conclusions. Q: But you disobeyed that instruction? Yes, says RW. Q: And you are supposed to include all matters in a report that you see fit. RW says he was acting with the full knowledge of the IPCC, and of his legal adviser. Labour's Chris Ruane goes next. Q: Are you sure minutes were not taken at the meeting with the deputy chief constables? RW says he took action points at all his meetings with the IPCC. Q: It is extraordinary that no one took notes? RW says it is easy to be wise with hindsight. He took notes in his policy file. Keith Vaz asks for a copy of those notes. Labour's David Winnick goes next. Q: Is there anything else Mitchell could have said at the meeting? RW says Mitchell was clear about what he did not say. But there is "doubt" about whether he gave a full account of everything said between him and the officers at Downing Street at the meeting with the Police Federation. That is why RW concluded that the officers were not guilty of gross misconduct,he says. Q: So you are saying there remains a question mark over what happened in Downing Street? Absolutely, says RW. Q: The Gaunt Brothers (a PR firm) issued a statement on behalf of the Police Federation officers before the meeting with Mitchell. Does not that suggest premeditation? RW says his investigation had a very narrow remit. He was asked to establish if the offices gave a false account of their meeting with Mitchell. Q: What is the worst thing that can happen to someone guilty of misconduct? A final written warning, says RW. Q: And what is the worst punishment for gross misconduct? Dismissal, says RW. Q: And is there anything less serious than misconduct. Management action, says RW. Nicola Blackwood, a Conservative, goes next. RW says he thinks there is doubt as to whether Mitchell gave a "full account" of what he said at Number 10 in the meeting with the Police Federation. In a later interview in the Telegraph Mitchell provided details that he did not give to the three Police Federation offices. On that basis you could argue that he did not give them a "full account". RW says he is surprised that the officers did not press Mitchell for more detail of what happened in Downing Street. Vaz is going on about the lack of a note. He seems surprised. Keith Vaz goes next. He asks if there was a minute of the meeting between RW and representatives of the three chief constables. RW says he would have taken a note in his policy book. Q: Were official minutes taken? No, says RW. Vaz says for a meeting with two deputy chief constables, and a legal adviser, somebody would have made a note. RW consults his notebook. It's his policy book, he says. James Clappison, a Conservative, goes next. Q: Did you make it clear to all three forces (represented by deputy chief constables in two cases) that there should be disciplinary action? Yes, says RW. He says he does not agree with their decision. But he understands their reasons. Clappison quotes from RW's report. RW said the officers gave an account of the meeting with Mitchell that was "inaccurate and misleading". Q: Your views were left hanging in the wind? RW says he works in a disciplined organisation. Sometimes you do not agree with every decision. But a collective decision has to be taken. Julian Huppert, the Lib Dem MP, goes next. Q: Several changes were made to you report. Why? RW says it is common to change the words of a report. Q: In every single case the changes made had the effect of softening the report. RW says he could not be sure. But the final report had to reflect the views of the appropriate authorities. Michael Ellis, a Conservative, goes next. Q: You thought the three officers should face disciplinary action for misconduct. But your junior disagreed. Yes, says RW. Q: You say the officers did not lie, but that they misled. What is the difference? RW says the different amounts to intent. If they intended to mislead the press, that would have been gross misconduct. But he does not think that was the case. Q: Your report went to the officers before it went to the IPCC. That was illegal, wasn't it? RW says he was acting under the directions of the IPCC. But clearly if you look at the regulations that is an irregularity. Keith Vaz is asking the questions. Q: It is up to the chief constables to submit the final conclusion, isn't it? RW says he had a meeting with representatives from the three forces. The IPCC had directed that they were not share their conclusions with the officers. On 31 July a copy of the report, without the conclusions, was sent to the authorities. On the following day he made it clear that there was a difference opinion over the conclusions. The chief constables had the final say. West Mercia and Warwickshire senior officers came back within 24 hours with a recommended conclusion. The IPCC wanted just one conclusion. Q: Is Andrew Mitchell owed an apology? Yes, says RW. Q: Has this had an impact on the reputation of the forces? Yes, says RW. Reakes-Williams (or RW as as will call him from now) confims that the final report did not include his original conclusion that there should be disciplinary action. Democracy Live is not working, but the parliamentary website is. Jerry Reakes-Williams, the Professional Standards officer for West Mercia Police, is giving evidence. He wrote the initial report on the conduct of the three Police Federation officers. He says the comments made by the Police Federation representatives did have the effect of misleading the public about that meeting. If he had thought officers had lied, he would have concluded they were guilty of gross misconduct. But misconduct is not the same as gross misconduct, he says. There is room for doubt as to whether Andrew Mitchel gave a "full account". On the balance of probabilities, he does not think the officers lied, he says. The hearing has started, but the live feed is not working. I'm trying a different source now. Last year police and crime commissioners were elected. They were supposed to hold the police to account. But, in relation to this aspect of the "plegbate" affair, at least two of the three police and crime commissioners involved have generally sided with the police. Ron Ball, the independent Warwickshire police commissioner issued a press notice last Tuesday saying the IPCC's handling of the matter had been "manifestly unfair". Three days later he put out a statement saying the statement made by the three Police Federation officers was "at best an error of judgment", but also renewing his criticism of the IPCC. He subsequently accused Andrew Mitchell's friends of "baying for blood". Bob Jones, the Labour West Midlands police commissioner, said in a statement that the IPCC had led to the three Police Federation officers suffering trial by media. And Bill Longmore, the independent West Mercia police and crime commissioner, put out a statement saying he was "surprised" that the IPCC has not taken over the investigation into the three officers. He then put out another statement saying he was keen to get the facts and would not comment further until after today's hearing. The Commons home affairs committee hearing on Plebgate will be starting soon. Here's the timetable. 2.45pm: Dame Anne Owers, chair of the Independent Police Complaints Commission, and Deborah Glass, the IPCC's deputy chair, give evidence to the Commons home affairs committee about the Plebgate controversy. At 3.15pm Chief Inspector Jerry Reakes-Williams, from Professional Standards for Warwickshire and West Mercia Police, gives evidence. At 3.45pm Detective Sergeant Stuart Hinton from Warwickshire Police Federation, Sergeant Chris Jones from West Midlands Police Federation and Inspector Ken MacKaill from West Mercia Police Federation give evidence. And at 4.30pm Chief Constable Andy Parker from Warwickshire Police, Chief Constable David Shaw from West Mercia Police and Chief Constable Chris Sims from West Midlands Police give evidence. I say "plebgate", but the hearing will not really touch on what Andrew Mitchell did or did not say to the police officers at Downing Street when they refused to open the gates for him as he was leaving on his bicycle last year. That has been investigated by the police, and a file is now with the Crown Prosecution Service which will decide whether officers will be charged. Instead this hearing is just about the conduct of the three Police Federation officers who met Mitchell to discuss the case and who then told journalists that he had refused to give a full account of what happened. West Midlands Police, West Mercia Police and Warwickshire Police decided not to discipline them. But the IPCC criticised this decision on the grounds that an investigation seemed to show that they had not told the truth about what Mitchell said. It was a small incident, involving four people. But what the Police Federation officers said that day helped to contribute to Mitchell's decision to resign as chief whip and it has become a story about whether police officers can be trusted, whether the Police Federation behaves responsibly and whether it is right for the police to investigate misconduct allegations against officers itself. (In other circles this is called "marking your own homework". As we in the press have discovered, this has rather gone out of fashion.) Here is some background reading before the hearing starts. • The press statement from Deborah Glass, the Independent Police Complaints Commission deputy chair, last week criticising West Mercia Police for not taking disciplinary action against the three officers and calling for that decision to be reconsidered. Here's an extract. The investigation by West Mercia Police concluded that although the Police Federation undoubtedly contributed to the pressure on Mr Mitchell and his decision to resign, none of the officers had a case to answer for misconduct or gross misconduct. The investigating officer concluded that while the federation representatives' comments to the media could be viewed as ambiguous or misleading, there was no deliberate intention to lie. I disagree. In my view, the evidence is such that a panel should determine whether the three officers gave a false account of the meeting in a deliberate attempt to support their MPS colleague and discredit Mr Mitchell, in pursuit of a wider agenda. In my opinion the evidence indicates an issue of honesty and integrity, not merely naïve or poor professional judgment. • The full six-page statement from Glass setting out her case in more detail (pdf). This give more details of what happened when Mitchell met the three officers, and why their account of it afterwards was misleading. It is clear from the recording that at the start of the meeting Mr Mitchell makes his apology, and hopes he can “draw a line” under the incident. He answers the question of “what he did say”: “The incident was very brief I complied with the officer and I picked up my bicycle but I did say under my breath but audibly, in frustration, I thought you lot were supposed to f***ing help us and it is for that I apologise and I am grateful to that officer for accepting my apology and I should never have said it and I will never do it again...” ... Immediately after the meeting the West Mercia officer, who did not speak during the meeting, is the principal spokesman when the officers speak to the waiting media .... When asked by a reporter if Mr Mitchell should resign he says: “I think Mr Mitchell now has no option but to resign, he’s continuing to refuse to elaborate on what happened, I think his position is untenable.” Immediately following this interview all three officers gave separate interviews along similar lines to various media. • The transcript of Mitchell's 45-minute meeting with the three officers on 12 October 2012. • A joint statement from Warwickshire, West Midlands and West Mercia police responding to the IPCC statement. Despite a thorough investigation under the supervision of the IPCC we do not believe that there is sufficient evidence to support the view that the officers concerned should face misconduct proceedings. Our view is that the officers have demonstrated poor judgement in arranging and attending the meeting in the first place. In light of this our position is that management action is a proportionate response. It is important to emphasise these points; i) Mr Mitchell has not made a complaint against police; ii) Had the IPCC decided at any stage during the investigation to treat this as a managed or an independent investigation they had the power to direct the forces to convene misconduct proceedings. They have chosen not to exercise these powers despite being invited to reconsider and reminded of their options as recently as last week. iii) At any stage during a supervised investigation the IPCC can direct further work to be done and they have not done so in this case. • The Police Federation's initial response to the IPCC statement. The Police Federation of England and Wales welcome the conclusions of the IPCC-supervised investigation into the actions of three Police Federation representatives, which found no evidence of misconduct. The IPCC had the choice at the outset of this investigation either to conduct an independent, managed or supervised investigation, and it chose to supervise. The investigation team has reached its conclusion and in accordance with statutory regulations, a decision has been made by the appropriate authority that there is no case to answer. Against this factual background, we are therefore shocked that the IPCC Commissioner, Deborah Glass, would then appoint herself judge and jury by releasing her personal view that she disagrees with the findings and asks the public to decide. This is done with the full knowledge that her status and title will naturally undermine the considered findings of the investigation in the eyes of the public, whereas in fact those investigating and deciding the case are the proper arbiters in this matter. • An account of the partial apology that the three Police Federation officers issued on Monday. We acknowledge the investigation's criticism relating to our poor judgement in talking to the media following the meeting with Andrew Mitchell, for which we take this opportunity to apologise. We would like to emphasise (as we did to the investigation) that in no way did any of us ever plan or intend to mislead anyone about what occurred during this meeting or otherwise. Here are the main points from the urgent question on Grangemouth. I have taken it from Graeme Wearden's business live blog. Graeme has been covering the Grangemouth story in great detail. • The UK government hopes that the Grangemouth petrochemical plant can still be saved, despite its owner Ineos announcing plans today to shut it down. Energy and climate change secretary Ed Davey told MPs repeatedly that the government wants the plant to stay open if at all possible. It would still consider a business case to provide investment to help keep the plant running. Davey also confirmed that detailed contingency plans have been drawn up to protect firms and customers from running out of fuel and chemical supplies. He will meet with MPs at 4.15pm to discuss the issue in more detail. • Many MPs are very worried about the impact on the Scottish economy, and the knock-on effects on the UK chemicals industry. Michael Moore, Liberal Democrat MP, called it "An act of industrial vandalism not seen in decades". • MPs were critical of both Ineos and the unions, for allowing the dispute to reach this point. Alistair Darling, former chancellor, said it would be tragic to lose the plant to an industrial dispute reminiscent of the 1970s, while Sir Malcolm Bruce said both sides were guilty of "a degree of intransigence". Tom Watson MP called for an independent assessment of Grangemouth's finances. • David Cameron has announced a review of green energy taxes after saying they had helped push up household bills to "unacceptable" levels. He made the announcement at PMQs, after Ed Miliband challenged him to say if he agreed with Sir John Major and Labour about the need to recoup money from the energy companies. (See 1.08pm and 1.44pm.) The Conservative MP Zac Goldsmith, a strong environmentalist, took to Twitter to criticise Cameron for going back on green pledges he made before 2010. • John Bercow, the Commons speaker, has rebuked Cameron for calling Miliband a "conman". Bercow made the point in an intervention during PMQs. Let me just say, I let it go the first time, the word 'conman' is frankly unparliamentary. The prime minister is a man of great versatility in the use of language - it's a bit below the level. We'll leave it there, it's a bit below the level. • Ineos has announced it is closing the petrochemical plant at Grangemouth following a bitter row over pay and conditions, putting 800 workers' jobs at risk. My colleague Graeme Wearden has more details on the business live blog. • David Davis, an ally of Andrew Mitchell, has has called for a royal commission to investigate the "crisis of ethics" in the police, as three chief constables prepare for questioning by a committee of MPs over issues raised by the Plebgate affair. As Rowena Mason reports, Davis said police should wear microphones and cameras to record their actions to address a decline in public trust – an idea that has won the support of Nick Herbert, a former policing minister, who said the idea was "worth piloting". The call came as Sir Bernard Hogan-Howe, Britain's most senior officer, admitted the Plebgate row had clouded the public view of Scotland Yard and had taken too long to deal with.Speaking on LBC on Wednesday, he said: "During the time this thing has been an issue, the Met has been performing better than ever. We've just got to live with the reality - the newspaper headlines, the fact that you're talking about it, clouds the fact that crime's coming down at its fastest for 30 years. • Mike Penning, a welfare minister, has admitted that the government does not know how many people the benefit cap is forcing into work, after a new study said the flagship welfare policy was not helping the unemployed or saving money. • The Ministry of Justice has announced plans to help control the cost of motoring, including "a crackdown on whiplash fraud, a freeze on MOT test prices and a scheme designed to reduce the cost of fuel at motorway service stations". • Danny Alexander, the chief secretary to the Treasury, has said that British businesses should do their bit to revive the economy by investing some of the £500bn they have in their reserves. Here's an excerpt from a press notice about a speech he is giving tonight. He will call on business leaders to invest some of their £500 billion cash balances - enough for 50 Olympic games - now to ensure the economy hits ‘top gear’. Had businesses increased their investment by just 10% in 2012, he will say, then the level of GDP would be £12bn higher - almost a whole percentage point growth in GDP ... Cash balances in non-financial businesses have risen by £14 billion in the last quarter and £104 billion since pre crisis levels - a 26% increase since the financial crisis. Cash balances now stand at over £500 billion. At the same time business investment is currently 25% below its pre-recession peak. • Government plans to go ahead with the next election on the basis of an individual voter register, as opposed to the current household register, have been given a boost after an experiment suggested nearly 80% of the electorate could be transferred to an individual register automatically. • The Office for National Statistics has published figures showing that happiness increased between 2011-12 and 2012-13. • Justine Greening, the international development secretary, has announced Britain is to provide a further £2m in aid to provide water, sanitation and protection to vulnerable Syrian children who have fled to Iraq to escape their country's civil war. Here is more about energy initiatives announced (or half-announced - see 1.08pm) by David Cameron at PMQs. This is from the Press Association. Downing Street said that details of the competition review will be set out next week in the annual energy statement to the House of Commons by energy secretary Ed Davey. An annual review of competition in the energy sector will be conducted by regulator Ofgem, the Office of Fair Trading and the new Competition and Markets Authority. The first review is expected to begin within weeks and report next year, said the prime minister's official spokesman. It will look at issues affecting competition and consumers, ranging from prices and profit levels to barriers to new entrants in the market, as well as how companies engage with customers. "With regard to the charges that are part of people's bills, we are of course looking across the board at what more government can do to help customers and consumers," said the spokesman. The spokesman declined to give any detail of sanctions which energy companies might face as a result, but said: "It would be wrong at this stage to rule anything out in terms of how we get more competition into the market." The spokesman said the review was agreed by both Conservative and Liberal Democrat sides of the coalition and insisted that the Government was not giving up its claim to be "the greenest ever". The prime minister's ambition to "roll back" green levies has also been discussed with Liberal Democrats over recent weeks, and details will be released in Chancellor George Osborne's autumn statement on December 4. Downing Street sources said that, if there was no policy change, green levies could rise from the current £112 to £194 - or 14% of the typical household bill - by 2020. Cameron wants action to reduce the impact of the levies, the source said. And here is what Labour are saying. This is from a party source. The prime minister is clearly in a panic over energy prices. He is refusing to take real action to tackle overcharging now. He wants to wait another year when bills are going up 10% as we speak. The Labour party has a comprehensive plan to freeze prices and reset the market. David Cameron's response is panicked and totally inadequate. My colleague Patrick Wintour has a shrewd point about David Cameron's comment about "green" levies. I put "green" in inverted commas because some of the measures Cameron was referring to, such as the Energy Companies Obligation, involve funding energy saving measures for the poor. You could see them as much as welfare measures as climate change measures. PMQ's - Verdict: Earlier I said that was a clear win for Ed Miliband. Half an hour later, it looks even more significant - a big win, that left David Cameron. Wit does not always trump bluster, but it did today. Miliband's opening question was very neat. The prime miniser said that anyone who wanted to intervene in energy markets was living in a Marxist universe. Can he tell the House how does he feel now that the red peril has claimed Sir John Major? And Miliband continue to mock Cameron effectively, particularly with his question about someone taking Cameron's advice to switch from British Gas only to be hit with a price increase from npower. Cameron's front-foot aggression seemed misplaced, and his final jibe, about Major being a "good man" and Miliband being a "con man", was marked by a touch of desperation (although it was also noticeable that he was not going on about Miliband being "weak" as much as usual). However, tone is not particularly relevant. At PMQs even the best debating skills cannot disguise a policy lacuna, and Cameron's real problem was that he did not have an effective response to Miliband's best question: Major proposes recovering money from energy companies via a windfall tax, Labour propose using a price freeze, so how would you recoup that money? Cameron does not have a full answer yet, and that's why he lost. But Cameron did have half an answer. In fact, he made three significant concessions on energy policy. • He said that energy bills had reached a "completely unacceptable level". I don't think he has ever said this so bluntly before. This represents a victory for Miliband,who has forced him into this position. • Cameron was clearly than he has every been before about wanting to get rid of the green levies that, according to Cameron, add more than £100 to fuel bills. "We need to roll back some of the green regulation and charges on our bills," he said. • He said he would apply a "proper competition test" to the energy market. If Cameron had adopted an air of contrition, and flagged these up as significant concessions, he might have been able to wrong-foot Miliband. As it was, they were rather buried beneath his bluster. But these announcements mark the start of a Conservative fight-back on this agenda, and we are bound to hear more about them in the coming weeks. Here is some more on what Cameron was saying about green levies on fuel bills. And here's is a bit more on the competition test Cameron mentioned. Labour's Kevin Barron says he wrote to Cameron about Lynton Crosby's involvement in public house. Four previous prime ministers would have replied to his letters. Why won't Cameron? Cameron says public health is the responsibility of the public health minister. Crosby's responsibility is the destruction of the Labour party. He is doing a pretty good job. And that's it. PMQs is over. Ed Davey is now replying to an urgent question about Grangemouth. I will post a full PMQs verdict shortly. Cameron says the proportion of people on zero-hours contract was the same in 2012 as it was in 2000. Julian Smith, a Conservative, asks Cameron to pay tribute to people working in the intelligence services following the Guardian's "reckless" intelligence leaks. Cameron says people in the intelligence services do not get the credit they deserve. "We cannot praise these people too highly". He says households in his area have lost £40 over the last two years, while in Cameron's constituency they have gained £6. Cameron says that if you look at spending per household, grant plus council tax, in Liverpool the figure is £3,122, while in his constituency the figure is less than £2,000. Nadine Dorries, a Conservative, asks about a free school application in her constituency. Cameron says he wants to put "rocket boosters" under the free schools policy. Labour's Tom Watson says fixed-odd betting machines turn bookmakers into casinos. Will Cameron ban them? Cameron says it is worth having a look at this issue. Crispin Blunt, a Conservative, asks about Osborne Constuction, a firm in his constituency. Cameron says he is glad to hear it is doing well. Osborne Consruction is also doing well all over the country. Yvonne Fovargue, a Labour MP, asks what's fair about the bedroom tax. Cameron says Labour have opposed every welfare reduction proposed by the government. They would have to find £85bn to fund this, he says. Cameron says the government wants an open energy maket. And Ed Miliband appointed five of the nine people of Ofgem. If the energy market doesn't work, it is Miliband's fault, he says. Labour's Meg Hillier asks Cameron to abandon the welfare cap. Cameron says Labour are the welfare party. Richard Benyon, a Conservative, says more than 300,000 new businesses have been registered over the last three years. Cutting regulations helps them. What more can the government do? Cameron says news out today shows there are more companies than have ever existed. Some 400,000 businesses have been created under this government, he says. John Bercow says the word "con man" is unparliamentary. He let it go first time, he says, but Cameron should know it is "a bit below the level". Cameron, a man of great versatility, should use another phrase, he suggests. Brian Binley, a Conservative, asks Cameron to reduce the impact of Chris Huhne's renewable energy measures. Cameron says Binley has a point. Green measures are adding £112 to bills. Labour's Jim Dobbin says 1.5m people in the UK are addicted to drugs like diazepam. They are victims of repeat prescriptions, he says. Cameron says Dobbin is right to highlight this issue. People who are not addicts have become hooked on these drugs. Snap PMQs Verdict: Clear victory for Miliband (or for Miliband and John Major, to give credit where it is due). Cameron is shifting his stance on energy, now saying clearly that the price rises are unacceptable, but he did not have a good answer to Miliband. More later ... Mililband said telling people to switch suppliers was a con. What would he say to someone who switched from British Gas to npower, only to see npower raise its prices. Cameron says Miliband left the energy market in a mess. Miliband is trying to con the public. The government will deliver for people. Miliband says John Major was right. Cameron is not standing up to the energy companies. The price rises will affect people who Cameron will never meet and whose lives he does not understand. Cameron says Major is a good man. Miliband is a con man. Miliband says Cameron is changing his policies every week. Ed Davey said green measures had nothing to do with price rises. And Cameron used to say vote blue, go green. Cameron says Miliband created the big six energy companies. He says he has Labour's briefing on its energy policies. Question 7 is: What would stop the energy companies increasing their prices in advance? There is no answer, Cameron says. And question 6 asks: What is to stop the companies putting their prices up after the freeze. The document says the public would take a dim view. Miliband says energy bills went down by £100 when he was energy secretary. Cameron says the price rises are unacceptable. But he is the prime minister, heaven help us. So he should implement Labour's policy now. Two parties working together in the common interest. Cameron tries to rise, but Miliband gets him to sit down. Let's do it now, Miliband says. Cameron says Labour's policy is price con. Prices would go up before and afterwards. It is a cynical ploy, he says. Ed Miliband pays his own tribute to the dead soldier. Cameron said anyone intervening in the energy markets was living in a Marxist universe. How does he feel now that the red peril has claimed John Major, Miliband asks. Cameron says he believes in intervening in the energy market. Bills have reached an unacceptable level. We have to have a conversation about what puts them up. Miliband is engaged in cynical gimmicks. Miliband says Major won a majority, unlike Major. Cameron now says the price increases are "unacceptable". So what are we going to do? Major wants a windfall tax. Miliband wants a price freeze. What would Cameron do? Cameron says he wants to roll back some of the green regulations and charges. We know who put them in place? John Major did won an election. He beat a weak and incredible Labour leader. Isn't that familiar? And he said Labour's policy was unworkable. There are four bits of a bill: wholesale prices; transmission costs; profits; and green measures. Cameron says there will be a proper competition test for the market. Julian Brazier, a Conservative, asks if it is a good time for those branches of the Police Federation that traduced Andrew Mitchell, and from Ed Miliband. Cameron says he wants to start by paying tribute to the police. But the police do need to make a "proper apology" to Mitchell. We all need to consider what we said at the time, he says, and he hopes Ed Miliband will consider this. David Cameron starts by paying tribute to a soldier killed in Afghanistan. He made the ultimate sacrifice, Cameron says. And he says, on a happier note, MPs will join him in celebrating the christening of baby Prince George. We've got PMQs in 10 minutes. Grangemouth and John Major are likely to be the key topics. Here's what Number 10 are saying about the Grangemouth closure. There will be an urgent question on the Grangemouth closure in the Commons at 12.30pm, Sky News is reporting. You can read all today's Guardian politics stories here. And all the politics stories filed yesterday, including some in today's papers, are here. As for the rest of the papers, here's the PoliticsHome list of top 10 must reads, here's the ConservativeHome round-up of today's politics stories in the papers and here's the New Statesman's list of top 10 comment articles. And here are five articles that I found particularly interesting. • David Davis in an article in the Times (paywall) says that the Independent Police Complaints Commission should be given more powers and that there should be a royal commission on policing. What we need is not a new regulator, but a tougher regulator. Earlier this year a parliamentary inquiry concluded that the Independent Police Complaints Commission “has neither the powers nor the resources that it needs to get to the truth when the integrity of the police is in doubt”. The Government should respond by giving the IPCC the powers and resources to outrank and overrule every chief of police in the land — to become a British “Untouchables”. Never again should a police force be able to delay or frustrate an IPCC investigation. However, while a tougher IPCC would ensure rigorous investigation of police misconduct, identifying such misdemeanours after the event is not enough. Nor is this a problem that can be solved by the Government’s recent Winsor-inspired reforms. The issues are bigger than pay, paperwork and pensions. This is a crisis of ethics. Britain needs root-and-branch reform of policing culture, a feat beyond the powers of even a powerful independent regulator. The Government should appoint a Royal Commission to investigate the conduct of the police. The lessons about what behaviour is expected from a British police officer should be instilled from Day 1. He also says officers should wear cameras and microphones to record their dealings with members of the public. The police put millions of innocent people under surveillance in order to catch a tiny minority of wrongdoers. Perhaps now it is time to make officers wear a camera and microphone while on duty. When they tried this in California, use of force by police officers dropped by two thirds in a year. This technology could also help to defend police officers who have vexatious claims made against them. • Jim Pickard and Elizabeth Rigby in the Financial Times (subscription) say Ed Miliband has told supporters at a private dinner that he anticipates a "big fight" with Fleet Street. Ed Miliband has put Labour on notice for a “big fight” with Fleet Street ahead of the general election, telling supporters that the newspapers are “less powerful now than they were”. The Labour leader, speaking at a private dinner for Labour donors, said the days in which newspapers and the news media could attack his party “without response” were over ... Recorded at a dinner at the Phoenix Palace, a luxury Chinese restaurant in Marylebone, the Labour leader said [his row with the Daily Mail about his father] was an emblem of how politics was conducted in Britain. “We’ve got to be willing to call these people out,” he said. “They are less powerful than people ever thought and they are less powerful now than they were.” • Daniel Finkelstein in the Times (paywall) says voters will not believe Ed Miliband when he says he can freeze energy bills. When I worked for William Hague back in the late 1990s, we were experimenting with focus group research for the first time. None of us quite appreciated how it worked. We would discover that groups strongly agreed with us on an issue — tax allowances, for instance — and we would try playing back the policy to those voters in the form of a promise. Yet the moment these same voters heard their own favoured policy read back to them by a politician they started to disagree with it. “That will never work,” they would say. Or: “That sounds fine, but they’ll never do it.” Or: “Yeah, but how are they going to pay for it?” They are also much more likely to believe that a politician might bring to an end something they approve of than that they will deliver something nice. This is one of the reasons, I am afraid, that negative campaigning is effective despite most people saying that they don’t like negative campaigns. • James Slack in the Daily Mail says the government is introducing a range of measures to cut the cost of motoring. Electronic signs are to be put up on motorways to expose service stations trying to rip off motorists with inflated charges. They are part of a package of measures unveiled by Justice Secretary Chris Grayling today to reduce the cost of motoring. They include freezing the MoT test fee for a car at £54.85 until 2015, and a review of the cost of taking a driving test and applying for a provisional licence. Grayling is involved because his department is taking steps to cut the number of fraudulent whiplash claims which drive insurance costs up. • Brandon Lewis, the communities minister, tells the Daily Telegraph in an interview that councils should lift charges for parking. Brandon Lewis, the high streets minister, called on local authority bosses to risk legal challenges by offering motorists free short-stay parking. In his first interview since taking on the high streets role in this month’s reshuffle, Mr Lewis said that councils must make high streets an “attractive place where people want to go” by not enforcing punishing parking charges. He also called for a change in the role played by parking wardens across the country, insisting that they are not there to “penalise” drivers. “If we want to see our high streets and our market towns prosper and do well we’ve got to make it easy and affordable to park,” he said. Ineos has announced that it is going to close part of the Grangemouth petrochemical plant in Scotland. The Press Association has just filed this. Part of the giant Grangemouth site is to close following a bitter row over pay and conditions. Owner Ineos has decided to shut down the petrochemical side of the complex, which is situated next to the firm's oil refinery. Workers were given the grim news at a meeting with Ineos chairman Calum MacLean. The news follows the passing of a deadline on a survival plan put to employees which asked them to accept changes to pensions and other terms and conditions. The Unite union said around 680 of the site's 1,370-strong workforce had rejected the company's proposals, which include a pay freeze for 2014-16, removal of a bonus up to 2016, a reduced shiftallowance and ending of the final salary pension scheme. Ineos said owner Jim Ratcliffe and other shareholders met yesterday to study the response from the workforce to their survival plan, and wanted the employees to be the first to know of any decision the company makes. It is not just David Davis saying the police should wear cameras and microphones to record their contact with members of the public . Roger Evans, a Conservative member of the London assembly, has sent out a news release saying he will be asking Boris Johnson to pilot the idea in the capital. Think of the role video cameras in patrol vehicles have played in helping ensure the safety of police and public, the next logical step is to extend these camera’s to the police officers person. Though this sounds like very futuristic kit, these wearable cameras are quite cheap and easy to use, and it’s not uncommon to see something like these police worn cameras in a local electronics shop. Staffordshire police have been equipping officers with this technology since 2010 and they not only help reduce bureaucracy as officers can avoid writing long statements, they have reduced complaints against officers significantly. Body Worn Cameras will represent great value for money to the Met, so let’s get moving with a full out trial of this tech. According to Evans, in Staffordshire the use of body cameras of this kind has cut the time taken to file incident reports by 22%, given officers an extra 50 minutes of patrol time per day and led to a reduction in disorder and anti-social behaviour. He says that there have been no complaints against officers wearing the cameras and that Staffordshire Police are now buying 530 of them. According to the Office for National Statistics, we got happier last year. Only a bit, mind you, but I suppose that's better than getting less happy. The ONS has published its personal well-being data for 2012-13 (pdf), and here are the key points from the summary. • Personal well-being improved in the UK between 2011/12 and 2012/13. Ratings for life satisfaction and feeling that the things we do in life are worthwhile increased on average, whilst anxiety levels fell. • The proportions rating their personal well-being as very high or very low fell between 2011/12 and 2012/13 in the UK, while the proportion rating their well-being around 7 or 8 out of 10 increased.• Among the countries of the UK, Northern Ireland had the highest average ratings for life satisfaction, worthwhile and happiness in 2012/13. A greater proportion of people in Northern Ireland rated their life satisfaction, worthwhile and happiness as very high (9 or 10 out of 10) than in any other country. • Among the English regions, the South West and the South East had some of the highest levels of average life satisfaction and worthwhile ratings in 2012/13. The South West also had proportionately more people than any other region rating life satisfaction, worthwhile and happiness as 9 or 10 out of 10 Why did people get happier? The ONS says it is not entirely sure. But the Olympics and the Diamond Jubilee may have been a factor. Reasons for the small improvement in personal well-being between 2011/12 and 2012/13 are not fully understood. Recent ONS analysis on ‘What matters most to personal well-being?’ (ONS, 2013b) found that the factors most associated with personal well-being are health, employment situation and relationship status. Where possible, these factors will be referred to to help contextualise differences in personal well-being across different areas of the UK. At the UK level, improvements in the labour market picture and special events such as the Queen’s Diamond Jubilee and the 2012 Summer Olympic and Paralympic games were suggested as factors which could have positively influenced people’s assessment of their personal well-being during the 2012/13 period (ONS, 2013a). Another Conservative MP has had a go at Ron Ball, the Warwickshire police commissioner who complained that Andrew Mitchell's friends were "baying for blood" over Plebgate. This is what David Ruffley told BBC News. [We're] certainly not [baying for blood]. We have the three chief constables in today and they investigated themselves, they said at the end of the day no disciplinary proceedings, but there is evidence they changed the story from being disciplinary proceedings recommended to no charge to answer. So first of all we need to know what was changed. Ruffley also said today's hearing would expose the "inadequacy" of the system that allows the police to investigate themselves. Nick Herbert, the Conservative former policing minister, gave an interview to the Today programme about the "plebgate" case. Here are the main points. I've taken the quotes from PoliticsHome. • Herbert said the reputation of the entire police was at stake. The vast majority of officers can rightly feel aggrieved that, actually, the service is tarred by this behaviour. And I think the failure of the service collectively to grip this, to demonstrate that they think that officers who give a false account is unacceptable or shouldn't be sanctioned appropriately, I think that is incredibly damaging to public confidence in the police, and it is up to them this afternoon and going forwards to demonstrate they are running the service in a proper manner. • Herbert said the disciplinary inquiry into the three officers should be re-opened. • He said a suggestion in the Times from David Davis, the Conservative MP and a close friend of Andrew Mitchell's, that the police should be fitted with cameras and microphones to record all contact with members of the public was "worth piloting". • He criticised Ron Ball, the Warwickshire police and crime commissioner, for saying that Andrew Mitchell's friends have been "baying for blood" over the affair. I think that's a spectacularly ill-judged comment given that these findings were made by the Independent Police Complaints Commission ... His job as the elected police and crime commissioner is to hold his force to account. Of course we want to get to the truth of this matter, but he needs to remember he should act in the public interest. Norman Smith (who interviewed Keith Vaz this morning) also says the committee will publish the first draft of the report into the conduct of the three Police Federation officers accused of giving a misleading account of their meeting with Andrew Mitchell, as well as the final report. The original report was written by Jerry Wreakes-Williams, who is giving evidence to the committee this afternoon. According to the Sunday Times (paywall), he was unhappy that his decision to recommend disciplinary action against the three officers were over-ruled by more senior officers. This newspaper has now been told that the senior officer who wrote the initial report on Mitchell’s Sutton Coldfield meeting, Jerry Wreakes-Williams, has privately expressed his disquiet about the actions of the superiors who overruled his recommendation that Hinton, Jones and MacKaill be disciplined. “He is not happy. But he has to accept the decisions of his superiors,” said a source close to the case. Keith Vaz, the chair of the home affairs committee, told BBC News this morning that the role of this committee was to establish the facts. I've taken the quote from PoliticsHome. We’re not a peace council, we’re a committee of parliament, and our role is to establish the facts and see where the facts lead. Of course there are others who wish to be making comments and wish to involved and it’s always helpful to have them as part of the debate. But at the end of the day what the committee will do is listen to evidence and come to a conclusion as to how we think the best solution to this agonisingly difficult problem is so that there is closure and people can move forward. It’s not just about one case: this has got wider implications about the integrity of and trust in the police and I think it’s important that we deliberate carefully, listen to the facts and come to a conclusion. Vaz said his committee may also want to take evidence from others before it publishes a report. According to the BBC's Norman Smith, the committee will report next week. Thank God for Keith Vaz. He may have been an indifferent government minister, and he would win an Olympic medal for vanity, but when it comes to chairing the home affairs select committee, and asserting the committee's right to stick its nose into any matter vaguely home affairs-related, and haul people in at short notice for a grilling, he has been hard to fault. Today we are going to get a classic example because the committee is going to take evidence from the three Police Federation officers accused of giving a misleading account of their meeting with Andrew Mitchell about the “plebgate” affair, the officer who investigated whether this amounted to misconduct, the three chief constables who decided not to take disciplinary action against them, and the Independent Police Complaints Commission, which decided that that decision was a mistake. In other words we're going to get a full inquiry into this controversy, concluded in the course of an afternoon. A few years ago Commons select committees were wary of engaging in this kind of micro-scrutiny, but Vaz and some of today's other select committee chairmen are bolder. It has been a good development for those who like to see parliament asserting its power. And, of course, it makes for great copy. I will write more about the background to the hearing before it starts. Although the session will just focus on what three relatively junior officers said after a meeting with the then Conservative chief whip, the ramifications spread much further, and what happens this afternoon may well affect the reputation of the entire police. Before that, of course, we've got PMQs. Here's the agenda for the day. 9.20am: Owen Paterson, the environment secretary, gives evidence to the Commons environmental audit committee on biodiversity offsetting. 9.30am: David Laws, the schools minister, gives evidence to the Commons education committee on school place. 10.30am: Christopher Jeffries, the Leveson inquiry witness, gives evidence to the Lords committee on public inquiries. 12pm: David Cameron and Ed Miliband clash at PMQs. 2.45pm: Dame Anne Owers, chair of the Independent Police Complaints Commission, and Deborah Glass, the IPCC's deputy chair, give evidence to the Commons home affairs committee about the “plebgate” controversy. At 3.15pm Chief Inspector Jerry Reakes-Williams, from Professional Standards for Warwickshire and West Mercia Police, gives evidence. At 3.45pm Detective Sergeant Stuart Hinton from Warwickshire Police Federation, Sergeant Chris Jones from West Midlands Police Federation and Inspector Ken MacKaill from West Mercia Police Federation give evidence. And at 4.30pm Chief Constable Andy Parker from Warwickshire Police, Chief Constable David Shaw from West Mercia Police and Chief Constable Chris Sims from West Midlands Police give evidence. 4.30pm: Cameron meets the Burmese opposition leader Aung San Suu Kyi in Downing Street. As usual, I’ll also be covering all the breaking political news as well as looking at the papers and bringing you the best politics from the web. I’ll post a lunchtime summary after PMQs and another after the home affairs committee hearing. If you want to follow me on Twitter, I’m on @AndrewSparrow.

results.webTitle: Plebgate - MPs demand apology from officers: Politics live blog

fields.headline: Plebgate - MPs demand apology from officers: Politics live blog
fields.trailText: Andrew Sparrow's rolling coverage of all the day's political developments as they happen
fields.bodyText: • Keith Vaz, the chair of the Commons home affairs committee, has said he is disappointed that the two Police Federation officers who were recalled to his committee this afternoon did not offer a full apology to Andrew Mitchell. (See 4.13pm.) Vaz told BBC News that the two were recalled because his committee felt they committed a contempt of parliament by misleading it two weeks ago. He welcomed the fact that Detective Sergeant Stuart Hinton and Sergeant Chris Jones apologised in this respect. In Mr Hinton's case he was very open and transparent and gave us that apology. Mr Jones apologised if he had misled us and, as we believed he had misled us, we accept that apology. But I'm disappointed they did not take the opportunity to we gave them to be unequivocal in their apology to Mr Mitchell and his family. Vaz also said that Mitchell's wife was present in the room as the committee took evidence. The Independent Police Complaints Commission told the committee that its new inquiry into the Police Federation officers accused of giving a misleading account of their meeting with Mitchell to discuss plebgate will be finished by Christmas. A very experienced senior investigator, who is not a former police officer, is in charge of the inquiry, the MPs were told. • Ministers have started to offer concessions on the lobbying bill ahead of a vote on Tuesday afternoon that might lead to proposals for a three-month pause in the bill's scrutiny and reference of the regulation of charities at election times to a special select committee. As Patrick Wintour reports, Lord Wallace, the minister handling the bill, has written to coalition peers saying he is willing to raise the threshold substantially to ensure smaller charities are not covered by the bill's provisions that restrict the campaigning activity of charities during an election period. Ministers have also proposed that scrutiny of the section of the bill addressing charity campaigning could be deferred as long as six weeks, so long as the rest of the bill continued as normal. • The British ambassador in Berlin has been called in for a meeting at the German foreign ministry to explain allegations that Britain has set up a spying operation in its embassy building. • Andrew Tyrie, the chair of the Commons Treasury committee, has said that more work needs to be done to establish the possible economic benefits of the HS2 high-speed rail project. Speaking after an evidence-gathering session, Tryie said: KPMG acknowledged today that their £15 billion per annum forecast return for the project has no firm statistical foundation. A lot more work is clearly needed. • The Fire Brigades Union has said that firefighters in England and Wales will stage a fresh strike on 13 November in their row with the government over pensions.That's all from me for today. Thanks for the comments. Keith Vaz is talking about the hearing on BBC News now. He says Stuart Hinton was open and transparent. Chris Jones only apologised on the basis that he might have misled the committee, Vaz says. But he says he was disappointed they did not offer a full apology to Andrew Mitchell. Here are the main points from the session. • The two Police Federation officers recalled to the committee refused again to apologise to Andrew Mitchell for what they said about him after their meeting with him to discuss the plebgate affair last October. There are have claims that they misrepresented what he said for political reasons, and that their inaccurate claim that he refused to elaborate on what happened at the gates of Downing Street contributed to his decision to resign as chief whip. The officers have already issued a partial apology, accepting that it was a mistake to speak to the media. But they have not accepted that they lied about the meeting, and they maintained that position today. "I cannot apologise for something I haven't done," Sergeant Chris Jones said today. Detective Sergeant Stuart Hinton said the same. • But the two officers expressed "regret" for the "distress" suffered by Mitchell. After the hearing two weeks ago they were criticised for not being sympathetic to Mitchell's situation. (See 2.41pm.) Today Hinton read out a statement on behalf of himself, Jone and Ken MacKaill (the third Police Federation officer who met Mitchell). Hinton said: Our position in so far as the meeting with Mr Mitchell is concerned hasn't changed. While the committee appears to believe that we are indifferent to Mr Mitchell's predicament and the distress caused to him and his family since the original incident in Downing Street. We are not, I am not. Each of us fully recognises and regrets that fact that such distress has been caused. We share the belief that the investigation into the original incident in Downing Street has already taken a disproportionate period of time and should be resolved in the interests of all parties without delay. Keith Vaz, the committee chairman, seemed to interpret this as a personal apology to Mitchell (when he asked a leading question about whether Hinton was apologising, Hinton said he was, for the distressed caused), whereas Vaz got Jones to say that Jones was not apologising to Mitchell. But Hinton and Jones were both essentially adopting the same position - expressing general regret for what happened to Mitchell, but not apologising for wrongdoing in this regard. • Hinton apologised to the committee for wrongly saying he had not referred to Theresa May as "this woman" in his conversation with Mitchell. (See 2.41pm.)And he apologised to May. I inadvertently gave an inaccurate answer to the committee on the 23 October. There was no intention to mislead the committee. I repeat my unqualified apology to the committee for this inaccuracy. I also repeat the unequivocal apology I made in writing as part of that same document to the home secretary. Whilst I believe that at the relevant point of the meeting on the 12 October [2012] I appear to have failed to bring the home secretary's name to mind, I fully accept that this does not excuse the form of expression I used in the meeting with Mr Mitchell and I apologise for that accordingly. • Jones told the committee that he did not mean to mislead the committee when he answered a question about his disciplinary record two weeks ago (see 2.41pm), but he said that he apologised if he misunderstood the question. It will be seen that on a proper analysis I do not believe that my answers misled the committee. My interpretation of the meaning of the questions has not changed. If, however, I failed to recognise the meaning of the questions I was asked, I apologise. If the committee was misled by my answers, then that was wholly unintentional. Do those amount to new apologies or not? Both men backed down over the allegation that they misled the committee two weeks ago. (See 2.41pm.) Stuart Hinton apologised clearly. Chris Jones was less apologetic, but he has written a letter to the committee, and there may be an apology in that. But the MPs on the committee are much more interested in a wider point - getting them to apologise to Andrew Mitchell for what happened to him. On this, the two officers gave ground a little. They said they regretted giving the impression that they did not care about the distress caused to Mitchell. But this seemed to be a reference to the distress caused to Mitchell by the plebgate affair generally. (Remember, these officers had nothing to do with the incident in Downing Street.) As for what they said themselves after their meeting with Mitchell, they do not accept that they deliberately misrepresented what he told them. And so, on that point, they would not apologise. Jones was more explicit about this (not apologising) than Hinton, although, as far as I could tell, both men were adopting the same position. I'll post a full summary soon. Labour's David Winnick asks Jones if he is absolutely sure that he has given the committee the information it wants. Jones says he has. And that's it. The session is over. Jones says it was not his intention to mislead the committee two weeks ago. But he misunderstood the question he was asked. If he mislead the committee because he misunderstood the question, he apologises, he says. Q: You have now given the committee all the information it requires. Jones says he would like to think so. Q: What is your disciplinary record? Jones says he has no findings of misconduct against him. But 13 complaints have been made against him. Vaz reads out what Jones's chief constable told the committee. (See 2.41pm) He says that in two cases action was taken. Jones says the action in relation to those complaints consisted of being given advice. That is not the same as a finding of misconduct. Q: What were those cases about? One was about the use of force, Jones says. The other was performance of duty. Q: What was that about? Jones says he was asked to secure a video. But when the video was opened, the wrong video was in the case. And Jones was criticsed for not checking the right video was in the case. Q: And the use of force issue? Jones says he pushed someone in the back when off duty, resulting in a minor injury to their mouth. Jones says he has submitted a written response to the committee. He is "disappointed" that he was not given a chance to respond to the committee before it published its report. It was not his intention to mislead the committee, he says. He says he "adopts" Hinton's evidence. Vaz says this is not America. A witness cannot "adopt"s someone else's evidence. Jones says he regrets what happened to Mitchell. Q: Will you apologise to Mitchell? Jones says he cannot apologise for something he has not done. But he recognises the distress that has been caused to Mitchell's family. He thinks it would be best if the investigation concludes quickly. Q: So you won't apologise for the distress caused? Jones says he cannot apologise for something he did not do. Q: You don't want to apologise for any distress? At the moment, says Jones. Chris Jones is now giving evidence. Vaz starts by repeating what he said to Hinton. Q: What do you want to say to the committee. Jones says he also has a written statement. Q: Have you any more to say? Hinton says he wants to say something on behalf of all three officers. They gave the impression they were unconcerned about Andrew Mitchell. That is not the case, he says. Q: So do you wish to apologise to Mr Mitchell and his family for the distress caused. For the distress caused, Hinton says. But he says he cannot apologise for something he did not do. Q: Is there anything else you want to say? No, says Hinton. Vaz says the committee has received a letter from Hinton's chief constable. The chief constable said it was a disrespectful term and that he would take this up with Hinton. Q: Has he done so? Not yet. Hinton says, again, it was an inadvertent error. Hinton is reading out his prepared statement. He says there were "inadvertent inaccuracies" in his evidence two weeks ago. He told the committee he did not remember saying "this woman" or "that woman". He had not listened to the tape for many months at the time. The transcript did not seem to make sense. Since the hearing, he has listened to the recording. He did say "this woman". And he was clearly referring to the home secretary. There was no intention to mislead the committee, he says. He apologises to the committee for that. And he says he wants to repeat the apology he offered in his letter to the committee to Theresa May. Keith Vaz, the committee chairman, starts. He thinks Hinton for coming. He says today's hearing will not cover what happened when Hinton met Andrew Mitchell. That is now being investigated by the Independent Police Complaints Commission. Instead, the hearing will cover what Hinton said at the hearing two weeks ago. (See 2.41pm.) If the committee is not satisfied with what Hinton says, it will report to the Commons, and the Commons will report the matter to the privileges committee. Q: I asked you about the comments in the transcript where you referred to "this woman". I said three times that this was a reference to Theresa May. You said it was a "typo". (Vaz is spinning this question out at quite some length.) Q: [Still the same question]. In your letter to us you confirmed you were referring to May. Can you confirm that? Hinton says he has a statement to read out. The session is about to start. Detective Sergeant Stuart Hinton is giving evidence first. And here are Guardian profiles of the Police Federation officers. The Commons home affairs committee is soon going to take evidence from two of the Police Federation officers who have been criticised for giving a misleading account of their conversation when they met him to discuss the plebgate affair. They are Detective Sergeant Stuart Hinton from Warwickshire Police Federation and Sergeant Chris Jones from West Midlands Police Federatsion This is what happened last time they appeared before the committee (with a third Police Federation officer, Ken MacKaill from West Mercia Police Federation). In a report at the weekend, the home affairs committee strongly criticised all three officers for refusing to give a full apology to Mitchell for what happened. We are appalled at the stubborn and unashamed refusal of an apology by Inspector MacKaill, Detective Sergeant Hinton, and Sergeant Jones to Mr Mitchell. The only remorse shown by the three officers is that relating to the choreography of their dealings with the media after the meeting. Their statement of the 21 October 2013, which they continued to adhere to during their evidence, apologises for the "poor judgement in talking to the media following the meeting" on the 12 October 2013. We believe this is an empty gesture of little comfort to Mr Mitchell and his family. No matter what their view on the case, we find it incredible that they cannot recognise the impact their actions have had on another person's personal life and career. But, although Hinton and Jones have been recalled today so they can apologise to the committee, Keith Vaz, the committee chairman, is not necessarily expecting a new apology for what they said about Mitchell. The two men have been recalled to apologise for misleading the committee two weeks ago in relation to specific elements in their evidence. The case against Stuart Hinton This is what the report said about Hinton, and why he was being recalled. (The bold is from the original report.) We also note that, despite accepting the transcript as having been a correct recording of the meeting, when DS Hinton was asked about his use of the phrase "we as you know as a federation have issues with the reform of this woman that the Conservative Party have", he claimed to have been misquoted and stated that he believed it to be a typographical error. In the opinion of his own Chief Constable, "It is clear that the phrase "that woman" was a reference to the Home Secretary and this is quite obviously a disrespectful term which should not have been used by anyone, particularly a serving police officer." We find it extraordinary that any witness, let alone a Chief Constable, should seek to correct the evidence given by another, particularly when that witness is a sworn officer, and given the nature of the investigation on which this inquiry focuses. The letter also specifically draws into question the integrity of the evidence provided by DS Hinton and states that his answers may have been "adversely affected by his mistaken belief that he may face a misconduct panel at some future time". We are appalled at the assertion that DS Hinton misled this Committee because of his belief that he could face a misconduct panel. All evidence given to select committees should be provided honestly and not be affected by forethoughts of any future outcome. It is an indictment of the quality of evidence provided by DS Hinton that, when specifically asked if the reference to "this woman that the Conservative Party have" was about the Home Secretary, he suggested that it was "a typo, to be perfectly honest". This can only be characterised as mendacious when subsequently he has informed us in a letter that he accepts that "the woman referred to in this sentence must be the Home Secretary. No discourtesy or lack of respect was intended in referring to her in this way. I offer an unqualified apology to her through your Committee if the transcript appears to suggest either. Having listened to the recording—I do not claim to have a positive memory of the precise circumstances over a year later—my interpretation is that I was somewhat uncertain in what I was seeking to say at this point, and appear to have failed to bring immediately to mind the Home Secretary's name." It is a serious matter to mislead a Committee of this House and DS Hinton will be recalled to the Committee to apologise for this. If he fails to apologise, that would constitute a prima facie contempt of the House. We are referring DS Hinton to the IPCC. The case against Chris Jones And this is what the report said about Jones, and why he was being recalled. We asked explicitly whether any of the officers concerned had been disciplined for misconduct on a previous occasion. Inspector MacKaill stated he had been previously disciplined for misconduct. We are still awaiting details of the case.
