Turning to the economy, Cameron says that when he went to his first G20 summit three years ago Britain had the most indebted economy, the highest level of household debt and the worst banking sector. In 2014 Britain will have the strongest growth of any European country. Cameron says Britain is leading the world in terms of the humanitarian response in Syria. At the G20 he held a meeting with other countries and agreed to push for unfettered access to Syria for humanitarian aid workers. He says he is also pushing for a political settlement. Cameron is speaking now. He says 12 members of the G20 signed a statement calling for a strong response to the use of chemical weapons in Syria. And on Saturday all EU countries agreed a statement saying there should be a strong response to the use of CW. Cameron says he will respect the view of parliament, and Britain will not join military action. But Britain will continue to gather evidence about the attack, and will continue to press for action at the United Nations. David Cameron is about to give a Commons statement on last week's G20 summit. Sadly, it is unlikely to include a reprise of his Hugh Grant party piece. These post-summit statements tend to be a rehash of what the prime minister said at the end of the summit at his press conference, but it will be interesting to see whether he has anything fresh to say about Syria, or about Labour's manoeuvring in the run-up to the Syria vote. The public accounts committee hearing with Mark Thompson and Lord Patten has now started. You can follow it on our media live blog. Here are the main points from the Nick Clegg press conference. • Clegg said he did not agree with Chris Huhne's suggestion, made in a Guardian article today, that the Murdoch press tried to bring down Huhne because of the stance he took on phone hacking. * Clegg said that he was "very disappointed" by Sarah Teather's decision to stand down as an MP at the election. He liked and admired her, he said. But he did not agree with her criticism of policies like the benefits cap. • He said that critics of HS2 were "spectacularly wrong". • He said that Labour party was "perilously close" to being in a position where people would not know what it stood for. • He said the recent overhaul of the common fisheries policy showed that you could achieve EU reform without having to threaten EU withdrawal. Q: Does parliament now have a permanent veto over military action? Clegg says the convention that parliament should have a right to express a view before military action is one that has now been set. But of course the government should reserve the right to act unilaterally in an emergency, he says. And that's it. The press conference is over. I'll post a summary soon. Q: George Osborne did not use the phrase "green shoots". Are you brave enough to use that phrase? Clegg says he won't use that phrase. It has been discredited. But he does think Britain is turning the corner. Q: You said last year that Cameron's position on Europe was a false promise wrapped in a Union Jack. Given that there is some evidence that Merkel could support Cameron's reforms, have you changed your mind? Clegg says the Lib Dems are "unambiguously" in. If Britain is not part of the EU, how will it be seen around the world? But the Lib Dems do agree on the need for reform, he says. For example, recently there has been a complete revamp of the common fisheries policy. That was something that he favoured for years. It shows you do not need to threaten to leave to secure EU reform. Q: Do you think it was a good idea of Labour's to say it would not support HS2 if the budget rose above £50bn? Clegg says the best thing would be for HS2 to stick to budget. In fact, it would be better if it came in under budget, he says. Q: Will there be an attempt to move the Lib Dems to the left at next week's conference? Clegg says the Lib Dems have changed "remarkably" since they have been in office, more than people realise. The debate on the economy will be very important. He says he is very against the amendments that would undermine the independence of the Bank of England, or re-open the fiscal mandate (ie, slow the pace of cuts). Clegg says "all powers to Messrs [Len] McCluskey and [Peter] Bone" because they show the need for the Lib Dems as a party to keep the others in the centre ground. Q: Do you think Huhne was right to say he was the victim of a vendetta by the Murdoch press because of the stance he took on phone hacking? Clegg says that's Huhne's view. It is not his view, he says. Q: Could Huhne make a comeback in the Lib Dems? Clegg says the last time he saw Chris he was not expressing any desire to return to politics. Q: Did the Syria vote change your view of Ed Miliband? Clegg says he was in favour of the government changing its motion, which he did. So he was "surprised" Labour tabled a rival motion making the same point. Q: At the Lib Dem conference there will be a debate on a motion calling for tuition fees to be abolished when money allows. Are you worried about that? Clegg says we know his position on tuition fees. The government has created a new tuition fee system. The early evidence suggests more, not fewer, pupils from poor backgrounds are now going to university. Q: Was it fair for newspapers to report on the Chris Huhne story? Clegg says he is not going to tell newspapers what to report. They play an important role uncovering wrong-doing. The "rigorous, muscular tone" of the British press is a model for the wider world, he says. Q: Are you no longer equidistant between Labour and the Conservatives? And how is your relationship with Ed Miliband? Clegg says he gets on with Ed Miliband, and David Cameron, just fine. They have all got young children; Cameron and Miliband have a good sense of humour. But what happens after the next election is a matter for the voters. And his criticisms of Labour relate to their policies. Labour are "perilously close" to the position where people do not know what they stand for. Q: Will the Lib Dems still go to Glasgow for their party conference next year if the SNP win the independence referendum next year? (The Lib Dem conference will be in Glasgow again next year, but later than usual, so it does not clash with the referendum.) Clegg says he will not base his travel plans on what the SNP are doing. Q: Chris Huhne said this morning he had been a victim of a vendetta by the Murdoch press. Should he have been paid for that article? Clegg says Huhne is now an independent agent. He won't comment on his pay arrangements. Huhne was talking about the need for politicians to make themselves available. The more politicians made themselves available, the better. Q: Will you start to differentiate from the Tories on the economy? Clegg says there are significant differences between the parties. At the Lib Dem conference next week he will speak on an economy motion containing many things the Tories dislike, such as giving councils more power to borrow. Of course there are differences, he says. Where they are united is on the need to tackle the deficit. In 2010 the country was teetering on the brink. He is "very proud" of the fact that the Lib Dems have shown such resolve under fire. Q: Sarah Teather said she was standing down because of government policies. Did you try to persuade her to stay? What do you say to other Lib Dems who feel the same way? Clegg says he was very disappointed that Teather made that announcement. He has known her for years. But he does not agree with her on all the issues she raised. For example, he thinks it is reasonable to put a £26,000 cap on benefits that can be claimed. But the Lib Dems have had to take difficult decisions. Q: Is there stil room for people in the party with Teather's beliefs? Clegg says he and Teather worked together on ending the use of child detention centres. They also worked together on the "progressive" measures for free nursery education he announced last week, and on the pupil premium. So there are many things of which he is proud. There are many achievements of which the party is genuinely proud. First question. Q: Do you agree with George Osborne that those in favour of Plan B have lost the argument? And does that mean Vince Cable has lost the argument? Clegg says those who backed Plan A have been proved right in the sense that chopping and changing would have been a mistake. It was important for the government to stick to its plan. But it did not do so "dogmatically". Nick Clegg is here. He starts by saying that no one is claiming the economy has recovered in full. But it is true to say that the economy is turning a corner. He is proud of the fact that the coalition, and the Lib Dems, did not buckle, and helped the economy through its darkest hour. He wants to see an economy that is more sustainable, not just in a fiscal sense, but environmentally too. The government has to invest for the long term. And that is why HS2 is so important. Britain needs a new, rebalanced economy, sustained by modern infrastructure. Those criticising the project are "spectacularly wrong", he says. (Presumably that includes the public accounts committee - see 9.30am.) No rest today. Nick Clegg is about to give his monthly press conference. I'm at Dover House in Whitehall waiting for him to arrive. It's not exactly packed. There are about 15 reporters in the room. • Ed Balls, the shadow chancellor, has accused George Osborne of "complacency" and trying to rewrite history. He was responding after Osborne delivered a major speech saying that the economy was recovering and that it was now clear that his austerity policies did not hold back growth. • John Kerry, the US secretary of state, has said that Syria could avoid a military attack by handing over its entire stock of chemical weapons to the international community. Speaking at a press conference with William Hague in London, Kerry said he did not expect this to happen. • Frances O'Grady, the TUC general secretary, has launched a stinging attack on the coalition, warning that its "war on working people" is threatening to divide the country in the same way Margaret Thatcher did in the 1980s. • Trade unions have announced a national day of action in support of workers who have been blacklisted after raising legitimate health and safety concerns. • Ed Davey, the energy secretary, has said that fracking for shale gas is not a "great evil" and can act as a bridge to a "green future" in the UK as long as it is properly regulated. • Vince Cable, the business secretary, has been accused of allowing the export to Syria of chemicals which could be used in the manufacture of weapons in breach of the government's own policy. Sir John Stanley, the chairman of the Commons Committees on Arms Export Controls (CAEC), said the export licences, issued in January 2012 should never have been approved. As the Press Association reports, Stanley is now demanding that Cable release the name of the company involved, insisting that he could no longer hide behind the need for commercial confidentiality. In correspondence with the CAEC, Cable said the licences for the export of sodium fluoride and potassium fluoride were granted after an assessment against both EU and UK rules - including whether there was a "clear risk that they might be used for internal repression" - concluded there were no grounds for refusal. John Cridland, the CBI director general, has put out this statement about George Osborne's speech. The economy is gathering some momentum and with business confidence on the up, supported by greater interest rate certainty, we expect growth to continue into next year. But to deliver a sustainable recovery we need stronger levels of business investment and trade to rebalance the economy, so we must get behind smaller firms who are ambitious to export. We have always said that deficit reduction should be at the top of the Government’s ‘to-do list’, but it must be coupled with an unrelenting focus on growth-boosting measures like infrastructure projects. Here's some Twitter comment on the Osborne speech from journalists and commentators. From the TUC's Duncan Weldon From the Sunday Times' David Smith From Coffee House's Isabel Hardman From the TUC's Duncan Weldon From Sky's Ed Conway From the Daily Mail's James Chapman From the Economist's Daniel Knowles From Glen O'Glaza Here's the verdict from the BBC's Stephanie Flanders on the Osborne speech. Here's an extract: Maybe things would have been better, the past few years, with a different chancellor at the helm. We will never know. Given this uncertainty, some economists would say it was complacent for Mr Osborne to leap on the recent momentum in the economy as proof that there could never have been better way out of the financial crisis than the one he has followed. That has not been proved. It never will be, either in favour of Ed Balls or George Osborne. But that economic momentum is real enough, and you have to bet it will translate into momentum of the political variety for the government as well. The argument about Plan B is over, give or take. The argument about the best way to rebuild UK living standards is just getting going. George Osborne is so fond of visiting building sites that the Guardian was able to produce a picture gallery last week showing him in an assortment of high-vis jackets. Today (without the high-vis outfit) he was at another construction site in the City of London where a development started in 2007, ground to a halt for three years, but has now resumed. It was a metaphor for the economy, he explained. As I said earlier, this was a heavy-duty economics speech. But while the content may have been all economics, the motive was pure politics – "Trust me, not Labour, because I've been vindicated." Somehow Osborne managed to deliver it without sounding too hubristic, but it was still a bold exercise in I-was-rightism. Here are the main points. • Osborne said it was now clear that austerity was not to blame for Britain's recovery taking so long. He said there were two explanations for the long period of flat growth. See 11.27am. Ed Balls has blamed austerity. But Osborne said that instead the Office of Budget Responsibility had been right to say that external inflation and the eurozone crisis were responsible for growth being so sluggish last year. Those in favour of a Plan B have lost the argument. The reason is simple: proponents of the “fiscalist” story cannot explain why the UK recovery has strengthened rapidly over the last six months. The pace of fiscal consolidation has not changed, government spending cuts have continued as planned, and yet growth has accelerated and many of the leading economic indicators show activity rising faster than at any time since the 1990s. If the fiscal multipliers were much higher than the OBR estimates, as the fiscalist story requires, this should not be possible. • He rejected claims that Treasury policies such as Help to Buy were fuelling a house price boom. House prices are down a quarter from their peak in real terms, and relative to earnings they are back at 2003 levels. Mortgage approvals are running at only a little more than half, and transactions a little more than two-thirds, of pre-crisis levels. That is why the Government’s Help to Buy scheme is a sensible, time-limited and necessary financial intervention to fix a specific financial problem: the dramatic reduction in the availability of high loan-to-value mortgages. The median LTV [loan to value] for first time buyers has fallen from a long term average of 90% to just 80% now. This change is not something we should welcome, it is both a market failure and a social problem – imagine if you’d had to find twice as big a deposit for your first home. 90% and 95% LTV mortgages are not exotic weapons of financial mass destruction – they are a regular part of a healthy mortgage market and an aspirational society. • He said the cost of living problem highlighted by Labour started when Tony Blair was in power. There is a focus today on the issue of household income growth. What is less recognized is that household income growth had started to slow sharply from the early 2000s. Annual growth in household disposable income more than halved from 3.6% on average over the five years before 2003, to an average of 1.7% in the five years after that. The proceeds of unprecedented global prosperity and rising trade were not finding their ways into the pockets of British families. Osborne said this contributed to rising household debt in the years before the crash. • He said Labour was to blame for the fact that Britain suffered so badly during the recession. GDP fell by 7.2% from peak to trough, almost twice as much as in the US, he said. In the past Conservatives have focused their attack on the last Labour government's spending record - allowing Labour to respond by saying this had nothing to do with the sub-prime crisis in the US that triggered the crash. Today Osborne said Britain fared badly because the entire economy was "fundamentally unbalanced" under Labour. What happened in Britain was the product of a pattern of economic development that had been fundamentally unbalanced and unsustainable for many years. The bailout of RBS was the biggest in the world because British banks had been allowed to become among the most leveraged and unstable in the world. The banks reflected an economy that had become, on some estimates, the most indebted in the world – with total private sector debt reaching 470% of GDP by the beginning of 2010. And the soaring budget deficit had its roots in an unsustainable increase in public spending in the eight years before the crisis – the second most rapid increase in spending as a share of GDP of all OECD countries. As a result the government ran rising deficits even at the peak of the boom and entered the crisis with a structural deficit that the IMF estimates was more than 5% of GDP, the highest in the G7. At the time most economists took a narrow view of sustainable growth: if inflation was low, and unemployment was close to its so-called ‘natural’ rate, then that was deemed to be enough. Clearly that was a huge mistake. We now know that truly sustainable growth also depends on sound public finances, well capitalized banks, healthy balance sheets, and a system of financial regulation that is alert to broader risks to the economy like asset bubbles and excessive debt. • He said government policies were having a "huge" impact in terms of helping people with the cost of living. The falls in mortgage rates that our plan has delivered are worth £2,000 a year to a family taking out a standard fixed-rate mortgage of £100,000. Our record increases in the personal allowance have already saved a basic rate taxpayer £600 a year, rising to £700 a year by next year. We’ve used billions of pounds to help with fuel costs by freezing fuel duty for over two years. Low mortgage rates. A £10,000 Personal Allowance. Fuel duty frozen. And soon tax free childcare. These make huge, positive impacts on the cost of living. • He said Labour plans to increase spending would have "disastrous" impact because interest rates would rise. • He defended Mark Carney's decision to issue "forward guidance" promising to keep interest rates low while unemployment remains above 7%. Some have interpreted more recent increases in gilt yields as a sign that forward guidance has somehow failed, but that is, I believe, a misunderstanding. I’d argue that market movements since the August Inflation Report vindicate the need for forward guidance: the counterfactual would have been even bigger increases in yields in response to positive economic data. And the evidence suggests that forward guidance is succeeding in changing expectations in the real economy: the Bank of England’s Inflation Attitudes Survey shows a marked fall in the proportion of people expecting interest rates to rise over the next year. • Osborne said he was "passionately" in favour of HS2. Here's the full text of George Osborne's speech. And here's the press notice the Treasury has issued. That sounded more like a presentation to an economics seminar than a political speech, but even non-economists like me could hear the key message buried at the heart of it: I was right, and Ed Balls was wrong. I'll post a proper summary when I've had a chance to read the full text. Osborne says the government will not be tempted by quick fixes. And he winds up. Osborne says the low interest rates the government has secured are worth £2,000 a year to someone with a £100,000 mortgage on a fixed rate. Osborne says house transactions are also below their peak level. And 90% mortgages should be part of an aspirational society, he says. And Osborne turns to claims that he is stoking up a house price boom. He does not accept this, he says. House prices are still 25% below their pre-recession peak, he says. Osborne says he rejects claims that the decision by the new governor of the Bank of England, Mark Carney, to offer "forward guidance" promising to keep interest rates low while unemployment is above 7% has pushed up borrowing rates. Osborne says that as the economy recovers the government must ignore the mistakes of the past. The main external risks we face today are these: the slowdown in emerging markets; the possibility of further turbulence in the Eurozone; and the risk that instability in the Middle East will push up oil prices. We remain vigilant on all fronts, and our economic plan gives us the resilience and stability we would need if any of these risks were to materialise. But we must be just as vigilant for any home-grown risks that could undermine a sustainable recovery. For macroeconomic policy this means three things: avoiding an unintentional and premature tightening of financial conditions; using the Financial Policy Committee and our new system of financial regulation to avoid the mistakes of the past; and staying the course with our deficit reduction plan. Osborne says the political pressure to change course was intense. But Plan B would have led to higher interest rates. It would have undermined the government's ability to pursue an activist monetary policy, he says. Amazingly, even with the evidence we now have, there are still those calling for the Government to abandon its economic plan in order to spend and borrow more. But to do so would be disastrous. The risks from unsustainable debt in our world may have abated, but they have not disappeared. For the UK now to set out to deliberately increase its budget deficit would be a signal to investors that we had abandoned discipline, at the very moment when –as I say – we are turning a corner. We would be back to square one. So the plan is working, but the recovery is still in its early stages, plenty of risks remain, and more years of hard decisions lie ahead. Our economy is turning a corner, but we must not take anything for granted. Osborne says there are two views as to why Britain's growth has been slower than hoped. The first is a simple – some would say simplistic – story that lays the blame mainly or sometimes entirely on fiscal consolidation by the incoming Coalition Government. Let’s call this the “fiscalist” story of the last three years – and it was advanced by advocates of a so-called Plan B. This argument claims that with interest rates at their lower bound, the fiscal multipliers – the impact of spending cuts and tax rises on output – have been much higher than anticipated, and the resulting impact of the consolidation on GDP growth far greater than forecast. The second analysis is put forward by many independent economists, including those who support our economic plan, the so-called Plan A. This analysis is that fiscal consolidation has not had any greater impact than originally allowed for in the Office for Budget Responsibility’s June 2010 forecasts. Instead, the composition and timing of the slowdown in GDP growth relative to forecast is better explained by external inflation shocks, the eurozone crisis and the ongoing impact of the financial crisis on financial conditions. Osborne says that in the last few months the argument has shifted "decisively" in favour of the second analysis. That is because the "fiscalists" cannot explain why the economy has strengthened within the last six months. Osborne says his economic plan is the right response to imbalances in the UK economy. Since the end of the recession the UK economy has grown by 4.3% - less than the US, but more than the eurozone. But employment has grown by more, which has not happened in the US, he says. Working age inactivity in the UK has fallen. But in the US it has risen, he says. Osborne turns to household income (the issue highlighted by Labour). But people do not recognise that household income growth started to slow in the early 2000s, he says. Britain was not earning its way in the world, he says. George Osborne is making his economy speech now. He started by saying that Britain was "turning a corner", but still in the "early stages" of recovery. He's not exactly pithy, is he, John Kerry? He certainly rambled on. But the US secretary of state addressed all the points put to him in his press conference with William Hague and, in his verbose, deliberate way, he was rather effective as a communicator. Here are the main points. • Kerry said that not attacking Syria would be more dangerous than attacking. Although he said that he did not know what President Obama would do if he lost the vote in Congress on military action, he also said that it would be a mistake for the US government not to order an attack. The question for all of us is what are we going to do about it? Turn our backs? Have a moment of silence here a dictator can, with impunity, threaten the rest of the world that he is going to retaliate for his own criminal activity because he's being held accountable? We live in a dangerous world as it is, folks. And that kind of threat is nothing different from the threat we face every single day. And if we don't stand up to it we will face it more and they will think they can intimidate anybody. I don't believe that we should shy from this moment. The risk of not acting is greater than the risk of acting. And everybody needs to stop and think about that hard. • He said the Americans were planning an "unbelievably small" attack on Syria. It would be similar to the American bombing raid on Tripoli order by President Reagan, he said. We will be able to hold Bashar al-Assad accountable without engaging in troops on the ground or any other prolonged kind of effort in a very limited, very targeted, short-term effort that degrades his capacity to deliver chemical weapons without assuming responsibility for Syria's civil war. That is exactly what we are talking about doing - unbelievably small, limited kind of effort. That has been engaged in previously on many different occasions. President Reagan had a several hours' or whatever effort to send a message to Gaddafi in the wake of Pan Am 103. (Kerry was wrong about the reasons for the attack on Tripoli. Reagan launched his attack in 1986, two years before the Lockerbie bombing.) • Kerry rejected claims that President Assad did not personally authorise the chemical weapons attack in August. In Syria only three people controlled the use of chemical weapons, he said: Assad, his brother and a general. Under any circumstances the Assad regime is the Assad regime. And the regime issues orders. And we have high-level regime that have been caught giving these instructions and engaging in these preparations with results going directly to President Assad, and we are aware of that. So we have no issue about the question here of responsibility. • Kerry said the evidence showing that the Assad regime was to blame for the chemical weapons attack would convince a court. I've personally tried people who have gone away for long prison sentences or for life for less evidence than we have of this. So I'm confidence about the state of the evidence. Asked whether Obama would publish further evidence of Assad's guilt, Kerry said he did not know if he would. • Kerry said Assad could avert an attack by surrendering all his chemical weapons stocks to the international community within a week. But he also acknowledged that this was not going to happen. • He said the US had "no better partner" than the UK Q: [To Hague] Now that parliament has voted against military action, is there any more the UK can do, like arming the rebels? Or are you limited to standing on the sidelines? And would you support US action without the support of Congress. Hague says the UK is still pushing for a political solution in Syria, while respecting the will of parliament. As for what the US should do if Congress does not approve an attack, he says that is a matter for the US. Q: [To Kerry] Will you release more intelligence information? And do you have intelligence linking Assad directly to the attack? (One report today suggests he did not authorise it personally.) Kerry says he does not know whether the president will release more information. But he has already released an "unprecedented" amount. There is a risk. It could damage your ability to disrupt a plot, he says. But members of Congress are being briefed on the intelligence. They can make a judgment, he says. As for whether Assad was personally responsible, Kerry says chemical weapons in Syria are controlled in a very tight manner by the regime. Assad, his brother and one general are the three people who have control of CW. But the regime is the regime. The regime, at a high level, was giving instructions for their use. That goes directly to Assad. The regime has a huge stock of these weapons, he says. That's it. The press conference is over. I'll post a summary shortly. Q: [To Kerry] Despite what you say people in the US and the UK do not favour military attacks. Why do you think you know better? Kerry says he would not claim to know better. Since Iraq, people are suspicious of what they are told. He understands that. He sympathises. If he were not in Congress, with access to the intelligence, he would probably feel the same way. That's why it is important for the administration to share what it knows. He knows why people do not want to see US troops coming home in body bags. "But that's not what we are talking about," he says. The US is not talking about going to war. It is proposing an "unbelievably small, limited effort". The US has taken action like this before, he says. If you want to send Assad a congratulatory message, you would support non-intervention, he says. If the world does not respond, something worse will happen down the line. Q: [To Kerry] In that interview Assad said Kerry's comments reminded him of the "big lie" told by the US about Iraq. What is your response? And is there anything his government could do to stop an attack? Sure, says Kerry. He could turn over all his CW to the international community within the next week. But he is not about to do it and it cannot be done. On the credibility issue, he says he has just given real evidence. As a former prosecutor, he can say this is evidence that you could take to a court. He has seen people put away for life on weaker evidence. He invites people to read the evidence for themselves at whitehouse.com. (You can read it here.) He says Assad sent scud missiles into schools. He uses napalm against his civilians. What he says cannot be trusted. Hague responds to the same question. Referring to Bashar al-Assad's recent interview denying responsibility for the CW attack, Hague says we should not believe what he says. They are now taking questions. Q: [To Kerry] How seriously do you take the new threats of retaliation by Assad? And would President Obama have ordered strikes by now if parliament had voted for them? Will Obama go ahead with strikes if Congress votes no? Kerry says that's a well-put question trying to get him to answer something the president has not decided. The president took a gut decision based on what was best for the US. He knew going to Congress would be tough. But he thought it would be important. He says he cannot say that, if parliament voted different, Obama would have ordered strikes by now. Congress is listening and considering the matter diligently. There will be a full House briefing today. The president will talk to the people tomorrow. Kerry says he met a friend here, an MP, who said people are still questioning the evidence. He says he understands the fear about this, influenced by what happened in Iraq. That is why the US administration has taken its time. It has been determined to be thorough with the intelligence. The president said he wanted the story told. And the intelligence community was told to release more information than usual. There was a risk in that. But there was a risk in not releasing information. What do we know? We know Assad's regime gave orders to prepare for a chemical attack. We know where the rockets came from and where they landed. They all came from regime-controlled territory and landed in opposition-controlled territory. We know from social media that reports of the attack came out as soon as the rockets landed. We can see the video of what happened. Then we know the regime issued instructions for the attack to be stopped. We know the regime was worried about the UN inspectors. And we know the regime shelled the area attacked for four days before the weapons inspectors arrived. Syria and Iran admit there was a CW attack. They just try to blame it on people who did not have the capacity to launch this attack. And how would they launch the attack from regime-controlled territory. The idea that they did defies logic, defies common sense. So how will the west respond? If we don't stand up to this, we will face more attacks like this. The risk of not acting is higher than the risk of acting, he says. Kerry says the US and the UK are both in favour of the US/EU free trade deal being negotiated. They believe it will create jobs and growth, he says. He quotes Margaret Thatcher saying the UK and the US are true friends. Kerry says that as he drove in to the Foreign Office he saw people protesting about military action in Syria. That is their right, he says. He says he understands why people feel like that. But it would be good to hear people saying to a dictator, "Keep your hands off chemical weapons, protect your people." For almost 100 years the world has stood up against the use of chemical weapons, he says. And the world needs to remember what happened when it stood silent. He mentions the Holocaust and Rwanda. There needs to be a political solution in Syria, he says. But the parties have to come to the table. If Assad believes he can rub out one part of his community with impunity, he will never come to the negotiating table, he says. The humanitarian situation is obviously "dire", he says. Some 5m people have been displaced. "This is a humanitarian catastrophe of global proportions," he says. The US is the largest donor of humanitarian aid to Syria, he says. That shows the US is not just coming to this proposing military action. For years it has been working for a diplomatic solution. Kerry says American and British leaders made it clear at the G20 that a strong international response was necessary. Kerry says the US/UK relationship has been described as special and essential. That's because it is. It was like that before the Commons vote. And it will continue like that afterwards, he says. It is about values. And the US has "no better partner" than the UK. The bond between the two countries is a "paradigm for internal co-operation", he says. Sometimes the two countries stand together against the oppressive steps tyrants take. He and Hague spoke about their continuing co-operation on a whole range of issues, he says. Kerry is talking now. He thanks Hague for his generous welcome and all of his efforts. He is returning to Washington this afternoon to brief Congress. He has had a very productive trip to Europe, he says. He mentions his "personal friendship" with Hague. Last night he had dinner with Mahmoud Abbas, the Palestinian president, he says. He says he is encouraged by the way the participants in the Middle East peace talks are sticking with the process. On Syria Hague says the position of the UK is well known. The government fully respects the decision made by parliament. But the UK still has four priorities. 1. Pushing for talks at Geneva. 2. Offering humanitarian aid. 3. Trying to strengthen the moderate opposition. 4. Taking a resolute stand on the use of chemical weapons (CW). Many countries have called for a strong international response to the use of CW. It is to the credit of the US that they are taking the lead. The Americans have the "full diplomatic support" of the UK. The UK has offered a total of £400m in humanitarian support. And it will be pushing at the UN for aid agencies to have unfettered access to Syria. There can be no political solution in Syria if the Assad regime is allowed to eradicate the moderate opposition. So he and Kerry discussed ways of continuing their aid to them, he says. The US/UK special alliance is, at heart, an alliance of values, Hague says. They continue to work together on a range of issues. William Hague and John Kerry are here. Hague starts. He says he and Kerry spent most of their time talking about Syria. But he starts by saying he wants to pay tribute to what Kerry achieved in getting Middle East peace talks going again. The press conference will focus on Syria, but it would be a shame if it goes by without anyone asking about today's Sun splash (paywall) saying that a World Trade Center girder, given to Britain as a 9/11 memorial, is now rusting in a farmyard. In the US, memorials featuring girders from 9/11 almost have sacred status, he said. They will be here soon, apparently. William Hague and John Kerry are about to hold their news conference after their talks at the Foreign Office. I'll be covering it. Today the Commons public accounts committee has published a withering report about the HS2 high-speed rail project. Shiv Malik has written it up for today's Guardian here. This morning Patrick McLoughlin, the transport secretary, was responding. He said that the PAC report was based on work done in 2011 and that HS2 was essential for Britain's long-term future. The simple fact is we have not built a new railway line north of London for 120 years, there has been massive growth on our railways in the last 15 years; 750m journeys to 1.5bn journeys, inter-city journeys, have doubled, and the truth is we can't not do anything. This is planning for the long-term future of our railways. This railway will be up and running in 2026. It is essential that we have good connections between our main cities. I've taken the quote from PoliticsHome. How do you give a speech welcoming economic recovery without sounding like Norman Lamont? By attacking Ed Balls and not saying anything about "green shoots", for starters. At least, these seem to be two features of the speech we will get from George Osborne later this morning. It's an important speech because economic recovery changes the political terms of trade quite significantly. Calls for a "Plan B" will fall away, or at least mutate into something different, and coalition ministers will assume that they will gain some kind of electoral payback (although the historical evidence on this is mixed). Osborne's speech should be a thoughtful one; given the time it's taken for the economy to turn, he's certainly had long enough to prepare it. Here's an extract from Patrick Wintour's preview story. Chancellor George Osborne will claim on Monday that critics of his economic policy have now decisively lost the argument and will say the economy has "turned a corner", and that a sustainable recovery is under way. But he will insist that it is premature to consider any slowdown in his deficit reduction programme in the light of better economic forecasts, saying it is vital to stick to the course he has set. "Even if the improving economic news eventually leads to an improvement in the fiscal outlook, the job will not be done," he will say in a major speech aimed at setting the parameters of the 2015 election. "More tough choices will be required after the next election to find many billions of further savings." A bullish chancellor will claim that critics of his deficit reduction plans are at a loss to explain why the recovery is now taking place. He will point out that the spending cuts are at their deepest now owing to the backloading of the cuts programme. I will cover the speech in detail. Later I will also be focusing on Nick Clegg's press conference and David Cameron's statement in the Commons on last week's G20 summit. Here's the full agenda for the day. 9am: William Hague, the foreign secretary, hosts a press conference with the US secretary of state, John Kerry. 9.50am: Frances O’Grady, the TUC general secretary, addresses the TUC conference. 11am: George Osborne, the chancellor, gives a speech on the economy. 11am: Ed Davey, the energy secretary, gives a speech on shale gas. According to the Sunday Times (paywall), he will say: “We can’t bank on shale gas to solve our energy challenges today or this decade.” 2pm: Nick Clegg holds his monthly press conference. 3.15pm: Mark Thomspon, the former BBC director general, and Lord Patten, the BBC Trust chairman, give evidence to the Commons public accounts committee. As Jason Deans reports, amid reports that ministers are planning to axe the BBC Trust and hand responsibility for governance of the corporation to media regulator Ofcom, MPs on the PAC will attempt to get to the bottom of who knew what and when about controversial settlements, including the former deputy director general Mark Byford’s £1m payoff. 3.30pm: David Cameron makes a statement in the Commons on last week’s G20 summit. As usual, I’ll also be covering all the breaking political news as well as looking at the papers and bringing you the best politics from the web. I’ll post a lunchtime summary at around 1pm and another in the afternoon. If you want to follow me on Twitter, I’m on @AndrewSparrow.

tags.description: Latest news from Syria as covered by the Guardian. News and stories about the country, people and politics

results.webTitle: MPs debate lobbying bill: Politics live blog

fields.headline: MPs debate lobbying bill: Politics live blog
fields.trailText: Andrew Sparrow's rolling coverage of all the day's political developments as they happen, including Andrew Lansley giving evidence to a committee on the lobbying bill and MPs debating the bill, and its impact on charities, at second reading
fields.bodyText: • Andrew Lansley, the leader of the Commons, has rejected claims that the government's lobbying bill will stop charities campaigning. On a day which saw the bill criticised by the Electoral Commission in the morning, and by MPs from all sides of the House during the second reading debate in the afternoon, Lansley made a distinction between charities and other campaigning organisations. Most charities would not be affected because charities are not allowed to engage in party politics, he said. The bill doesn't constrain the ability of charities to campaign. Look back at 2010 - only two charities registered for the purposes of expenditure on electoral purposes and spent very little ... So the idea that charities are in any way constrained I think is completely wrong. But the bill would constrain other third-party groups that campaigned in elections, he said. That was the point; it was meant to. (An analysis shows that Unison is the organisation most likely to be affected by the news rules - see 1.04pm.) In electoral law already there is a very clear provision that if third parties wish to engage in expenditure the intent or effect of which is to procure electoral success, that they are beyond a certain point required to register with the Electoral Commission in relation to that expenditure and there are limits on that expenditure and that expenditure is controlled. All the bill does, which is the right thing to do, is to make sure that the limit is a more appropriate limit for the future and doesn't allow those third parties to engage in distorting activity in relation to elections. It extends the definition of controlled expenditure so that it includes advertising, rallies and suchlike as well as election material and it dis-aggregates that into parliamentary constituencies so that third parties cannot disproportionately concentrate their spending in individual constituencies - all of which I think are perfectly rational ... The point is if you are undertaking expenditure for electoral purposes you should expect to be regulated and there should be proper account made of it. But Labour's Angela Eagle said the bill was "one of the worst pieces of legislation I've seen any government produce in a very long time". The bill is bound to get a second reading when MPs vote on it at 7pm tonight. There are bound to be rebellions and concessions as the bill continues its passage through parliament, but at this stage it is hard to be sure quite how much damage the bill's opponents will be able to inflict. • William Hague, has rejected Labour calls for Iran to be included in Syrian peace talks, saying the Iranian regime was actively engaged in assisting widespread murder by the Assad regime, and had not yet expressed support for a transitional government in Syria. • Sir Hugh Orde, the president of the Association of Chief Police Officers, has said that the European arrest warrant is an "essential weapon" for police. • Justine Greening, the international development secretary, has said she was "absolutely gutted" to miss last week's vote on Syria. She voted against the Labour amendment, but missed this main vote. This is how she explained it on the World at One. We were in Parliament and indeed I has having a meeting with Mark Simmonds, Foreign Office minister, at the time, between votes, I voted in the first vote. Unfortunately for us we were in a room that it proved, apparently, that you cannot hear the division bell from. We were unaware the vote had been called because we were unable to hear the division bell. This is the key bell that MPs have relied on literally for 150 years to tell them what's going on in Parliament. It was deeply unfortunate and I was absolutely gutted to have missed the vote. • The Commons authorities have released figures showing their have been thousands of attempts to access pornography through computers on the parliamentary network. I've already quoted what the Public Relations Consultants Association (see 11.13am) and the Chartered Institute of Public Relations have said about this bill. This is from Iain Anderson, deputy chairman of the Association of Professional Political Consultants (APPC) This is a muddled mistake. As currently drafted it will do precisely nothing to increase lobbying transparency. Indeed, the only thing the government will achieve by pushing forward with the bill in its current draft is to unite all common sense and reason against it.” In two key respects it falls woefully short of achieving its aims. In registering only third-party consultants - and amongst them only those who conduct a "substantial" proportion of their work as lobbying business - it will not even succeed in capturing all agency lobbyists - the very lowest benchmark of success that the Government has set itself with this unambitious bill. Secondly, it will not even cover backbench MPs, who by all accounts are powerful actors in the policy-making process. In other words all lobbyists will be free to lobby the majority of MPs without ever having to register: the idea that you can meet with, for example, the Leader of the Opposition or a select committee chairperson and for this not to require registration renders this bill a laughing stock." We want to see a properly drafted bill that captures all lobbying but which doesn’t get in the way of legitimate democratic activity. This is not a difficult thing to achieve aided greatly by the fact that is a wide cross-section of opponents to this bill who are ready to work with the Government to make this a reality." The APPC wants to see all professional lobbying governed by a statutory lobbying register. The APPC has been operating our own register for nearly 20 years and yet we had absolutely no engagement with the government until this bill was produced. This is despite the fact that the industry had worked hard to produce a definition and a framework that would be workable and a registration process to provide proof of concept as to how a system could operate effectively. In the light of Shaheen's article in the New Statesman about a new party to the left of Labour (see 3.54pm), it is worth flagging up, for anyone who missed it, what the Unite general secretary Len McCluskey was saying at the end of his interview with John Harris in the Guardian on Saturday. [McCluskey] mentions the four senior Labour figures who quit Labour in 1981 and founded the Social Democratic Party. "There were things happening in the Labour party that they profoundly disagreed with, and they left and created their own party. Now, that's politics for you…" And does he think that might happen to the people he calls Blairites? "Or it could be the outcome for those individuals on the left of the party who believe, 'Well, we're getting nowhere.'" Including him? "I'm the general secretary of Unite. In our rule book, we're affiliated to the Labour party. And for that to change, our rules would have to change. We're not there yet. But the Labour party has no God-given right to exist. Ed Miliband's challenge is to demonstrate to ordinary working people – including trade unionists – that it's their party." This is a big moment, in other words. "It's absolutely huge." And he doesn't rule out Unite eventually taking its ball elsewhere? "I wouldn't rule anything out," he says. "In extraordinary times, extraordinary things happen. In my view, students of politics in 50 years' time may well look back on this period and realise just how incredibly important it was." Though he is working hard for a Labour victory in 2015, he thinks this will be possible only with "a radical alternative to austerity". If the Tories win, he tells me, "I fear for the existence of the Labour party. None of us know what would happen after a defeat of that nature. And it won't necessarily be the normal process of the leader stepping down, and a new leader taking over." As he says, extraordinary times. McCluskey has dropped vague hints before about Unite leaving Labour, but this was his clearest hint yet that he's considering the idea. Here's a short afternoon reading list. • Salman Shaheen at the New Statesman says Left Unity could become Labour's Ukip. In response to an appeal by [Ken] Loach, almost 10,000 people have signed up to the Left Unity campaign to form a new party of the left, with around 100 local groups springing up organically across the country. While for many this is not their first shot at uniting a fractured left and the painful experiences of the Socialist Alliance and George Galloway’s Respect are still fresh in their mind, there is a sense that there is something different about Left Unity. Undominated by a central charismatic figure, and uncontrolled by a single Trot group such as the SWP, Left Unity is a movement that is genuinely being built from the bottom up by local activists sick of austerity and fearful of the future of the NHS. As Left Unity moves towards its founding conference at London’s Royal National Hotel on 30 November, the task of harmonising such a large choir of angry voices will not be easy. But the space is certainly there for a left-wing party to fill ... The space is there to the left, the votes are there, and if Labour will not fill it, then Left Unity will. • Paul Goodman at ConservativeHome says Michael Gove is only one point behind Boris Johnson in a poll of ConHome readers on who should be the next Tory leader. Boris maintains his narrow one point lead over Gove, and May's recent advance inches further forward. The Mayor has risen slightly after falling back nine points last month: being a bit out of the news is the best explanation for the recent drop. The explanation of Gove's rise must be that he is seen by respondents as one of the Government's most effective Ministers. And David Cameron has also been talking about Syria. He told broadcasters that his decision not to hold another vote on military intervention did not mean Britain was not offering humanitarian help to the country. I think parliament spoke very clearly and it’s important to respect the view of parliament and so I’m not planning to return to parliament to ask again about British military action. That doesn’t mean we do nothing on Syria – we are already the second largest aid donor ... Britain has spent over £300m, our aid has saved tens of thousands of lives. I’ve been to Jordan and seen one of the refugee camps myself, and I know how much money and how much effort is going in. Obviously it’s difficult to get some of the aid into Syria itself and we must continue to try and crack these problems, but Britain, as ever, is a world leader on helping those who need help, and the people of Syria are right at the front of that now. I've taken the quotes from PoliticsHome. Turning away from the lobbying bill, I see that David Miliband has written an article for the Financial Times about Syria (subscription). It is mostly about the humanitarian crisis there, and Miliband does not express a view on whether or not MPs were right to vote against military intervention last week. But he does warn against disengagement. The humanitarian priorities should be clear: reach those in Syria caught in the crossfire or its aftermath, and shore up the livelihoods of those in neighbouring countries. For those in the country, non-governmental organisations cannot stop the killing, but they can staunch the dying. That requires safe access for food and medical help, across the country and across combat lines. For doctors to fear reprisals for treating the wounded – and I have heard such concerns myself from Syrian surgeons – takes us back to the dark ages. In neighbouring countries, there is desperate human need and growing political pressure. The host governments at the local and national levels, especially in Jordan, Lebanon and Iraq, cannot be expected to manage population flows on this scale alone. Their demands for help should be heard. It is clear that, while international engagement is decreasingly popular in the advanced democracies, a multipolar world makes it increasingly necessary. Humanitarian intervention is about human need, not political sides – but it has political consequences. There is capacity to save more lives, but this needs resources and political will. The drums of war are reason to redouble humanitarian efforts, not forget them. So, where does that leave us? Angela Eagle described the lobbying bill as one of the worst pieces of legislation that she had seen for ages and, on the basis of what we have seen today and over the last few weeks, she clearly has a point. It has received an uncharacteristically withering verdict from the Electoral Commission, the regulator that is supposed to enforce it. The charity sector and others affected by part 2 are strongly and, it seems, universally opposed (as Eagle said, if Guido Fawkes and Owen Jones are agreed that that it is flawed, there must be something wrong) and organisations representing lobbyists are also distinctly unimpressed. Even Tory MPs were struggling to show much enthusiasm. And yet? Listening to the debate, I did not get the impression (rather to my surprise) that the government is going to be hit by a massive revolt. Tonight, at 7pm, MPs will vote on the second reading. David Davis hinted that he will vote against the programme motion (the one saying how many days should be set aside for debate), but I don't recall other coalition MPs declaring that they would defy the whip tonight. On bills like this the rebellions come later on and, with all MPs entitled to take part in the three committee stage debates next week, there are bound to be some government revolts. But over what? The opponents do not seem to be coalescing around a key demand that could form the basis of a government-defeating amendment. Some of those most unhappy this afternoon were raising concerns (like the potential impact on the work of MPs, and the Bill of Rights) of little interest to others. And the government does not seem to be in a mood to give in. What was most revealing about Andrew Lansley's speech was the bit when he explained the thinking behind the bill. The curbs on third-party campaigning have not been introduced by accident. They are there because the government believes in them. There are bound to be some sort of concessions but, based on what we've heard today, there is no reason to believe they will be significant. Many Labour MPs have been expressing their opposition to the lobbying bill on Twitter. Here are two Tories who have been sounding off too. Eagle says the government has given no reason why unions, but not other membership organisations, should have to endure these cumbersome regulations. Free trade unions are part of a vibrant society. The attempt to interfer with their work is another sinister aspect of the bill, she says. And that's it. The two opening speeches have finished. Eagle says that officials have been unable to explain the reasons for part 3 of the bill (the section dealing with unions). It has been said that it came out of a meeting between David Cameron and Nick Clegg, she says. Gavin Shuker, the Labour MP, says the government wants better information about union memberships. But we know how many members unions have. And we know how many members Labour and the Lib Dems have. The one organisation that will not release information about its members is the Conservative party, he says. Eagle says that Lansley's speech made it clear that the government deliberately wants to gag charities. The flaws in the bill are not just the product of poor drafting, she says. She says third parties accounted for less than 10% of election spending in 2010. Eagle says the bill is a threat to the Big Society. Tories may not like 38 Degrees, she says. But we live in a democracy and they are entitled to campaign. The TaxPayers' Alliance is among the groups protesting about it, he says. Even Guido Fawkes and the leftwing commentator Owen Jones are united in opposing it, she says. Angie Bray, a Conservative, says 38 Degrees is a lobbying organisation. Lobbying is what it does. Eagle says that Bray's government think it is a third party organisation. Eagle says the bill is so bad it has united transparency campaigners and lobbyists in condemning it. Labour will table amendments calling for a universal register, backed by effective sanctions. And it will close the loophole than enables Lynton Crosby to operate at the heart of Number 10 without declaring his clients. Eagle turns to part 1, the section dealing with lobbying. Only this government could introduce a bill on lobbying that would not affect Lynton Crosby, she says. And only the government could produce a bill that would not affect a lobbyist lobbying an MP on the lobbying bill. She says the bill will cover fewer than one fifth of people working in the £2bn lobbying industry. It would not cover any of the lobby scandals that have hit this parliament, she says. And she says the new register will be less extensive than the current voluntary register that operates. Labour's Gavin Shuker says that under the bill Hope Not Hate, which produces information about far-right candidates standing at elections, would be allowed to spend just a fraction of the BNP. Eagle says Lansley has provided an abject lesson in how not to produce legislation. Lansley should be ashamed of the bill, she says. Labour's Seema Malhotra says this bill could lead to elections being reduced to just political parties talking to each other. Eagle says that is a powerful point. Graham Allen says standing orders should be changed to say that pre-legislative scrutiny should be standard practice, save for bills introduced in an emergency. If that had happened in this case, there might be consensus on the bill, he says. Eagle says the political and constitutional reform committee is so concerned about this bill that it has had to hold emergency hearing over the recess. Graham Allen, the committee chairman, described the bill as a "dog's breakfast", she says. That prompted a response from the Tory MP Douglas Carswell that Allen was wrong because "far more thought has gone into pet nutrition", she says. Labour's Dennis Skinner says the Lib Dems are supporting this bill because they do not want the National Union of Students campaigning against them over tuition fees in 2015. "What a scandal," he says. Eagle says Skinner has made a good point. Labour's Stephen Doughty says he was involved in the Make Poverty History campaign in 2005. He says that, under the terms of the bill, campaigns like that could be stopped. Eagle agrees. That is what the Electoral Commission has said, she says. David Davis, the Conservative MP, says Eagle has criticised the "haste" with which the bill has been introduced. In relation to part 2, he tends to agree, he says. He asks if Labour will be voting against the programme motion. By convention, constitutional bills like this one are not subject to the guillotine, he says. (That seems to be a hint that Davis will vote against the programme motion.) Angela Eagle, the shadow leader of the Commons, is speaking now. This is one of the worst pieces of legislation I've seen any government produce in a very long time. Lansley has been landed with this "risible" bill because other ministers have been reluctant to touch this bill "with a barge pole". She says the bill is hurried, badly drafted, and a combination of the sinister and the partisan. Labour's Paul Flynn says the government should study the virtues of humility. Eagle says that's good advice. She says the bill could be summed up as "furious displacement activity" from a government that hopes the public will not notice its other problems. Lansley has now finished. The Labour MP John Denham says he thinks Lansley had an even harder time than he did over the health bill. Lansley is now turning to part 3 of the bill. Unions have to keep an accurate register of their members. The bill will build on this, he says. The extra burden on unions will be very modest, he says. Lansley says the bill will force third parties spending significant sums campaigning for one party to need approval from that party. This will increase transparency, he says. Graham Allen, the Labour chair of the political and constiutional reform committee, says the new rules cover staffing costs. If these costs are included in the defintion of election spending, they will not be able to use staff for other campaigning. Lansley says charities generally do not engage in party political campaigning. If they do engage in election campaigning, they should expect to be regulated. Lansley says the bill will not stop charities campaigning during election periods. It will just limit how much they can spend on election-related spending. Andrew George, a Lib Dem, says he used to work for a charity before becoming an MP. Will Lansley agree to delay the bill so the concerns of the Electoral Commission and charities can be addressed? Lansley says there are existing uncertainties in electoral law about what constitutes spending for electoral purposes. He suggests the new rules will make little difference. (But the Electoral Commission disagrees. I posted a link to its briefing for MPs earlier. See 10.32am. This is what it says about how the bill defines election spending. The current PPERA rules on non-party campaigning are relatively narrow in scope (only covering ‘election material’) and the definition of what is covered is relatively clear, so we are able to produce guidance that builds on the legislation. However, it can still be hard for campaigners to understand what activity is regulated. For instance, activity by charities (which are not allowed to be party political under charity law) can be covered. A common example is where an organisation invites prospective candidates at an election to say whether they support its views, and then issues a leaflet setting out the names of candidates who have expressed support. This will be controlled as election material if it is distributed to the public, even though those producing the material may argue that their aim is only to comment on public policy, or to influence politicians’ agendas, rather than to persuade the public to vote in a certain way. The Bill makes major changes to the current rules. The new rules are closely modelled on those that currently apply to political parties, but will have to be read and applied in a quite different context because third parties are so different from political parties ... This will be particularly challenging for campaigners because of the need to apply the definition of ‘election purposes’, which is new and untested in the context of non-party campaigning.) Lansley says charities will not be affected because they do not engage in party political activity. But some charities set up political wings that are not charities. And some campaigning is done by groups that are thought of as charities but which are not charities, he says. He says Labour acknowledges this in its amendment to the motion. Here's the text of the Labour amendment. That this House affirms its belief in the need for greater transparency in the lobbying industry and in British politics, and considers that there should be a universal register of all professional lobbyists backed by a code of conduct and sanctions, clear rules on third party campaigning, and real reform to get the big money out of politics; but declines to give a Second Reading to the Transparency of Lobbying, Third Party Campaigning and Trade Union Administration Bill because the proposals on lobbying cover only a tiny minority of the industry and will make lobbying less transparent, and the proposals on third party campaigning amount to a gag on charities and campaigners who have a democratic right to participate in important debates in the run up to elections; and strongly believes that the publication of such a Bill should have been preceded by a full process of pre-legislative scrutiny and consultation with affected parties. Labour's Chris Bryant says that, under the bill, every lawyer and in-house PR official at BSkyB would be able to meet ministers without having to register as a lobbyist. Only someone working for an outside firm would have to register. How can that be right? Lansley says that if people learn that BSkyB staff have had a meeting with ministers, they can guess that those staff were lobbying for BSkyB. Kevin Barron, the Labour chair of the Commons standards committee, says his committee met this morning to agree a short report saying the bill should be amended to ensure that MPs are not covered by it. Lansley says that the government will take notice of the committee's concerns. Labour's John Denham says the bill was introduced after the controversy about Lynton Crosby working for the cigarette industry at the same time as advising ministers to drop plans to ban branded tobacco packaging. Lansley says he was health secretary when the government consulted on cigarette packaging. He is in a position to say that Denham is talking "tosh". Sir Gerald Howarth, a Conservative, says the first part of the bill underlines the Bill of Rights, saying what is said in the Commons cannot be questioned in the courts. But the bill also legislates for lobbying. What would happen if Howarth were to raise an issue relating to defence that was not related to his constituency? Would he be covered? Lansley says the government does not want the bill to cover the normal activities of an MP. If there is any doubt, the government will amend the bill to make this clear. (Kevin Barron, the chair of the Commons standards committee, has also raised this concern.) John Hemming, a Lib Dem MP, asks if MEPs or councillors would be covered by the bill. Would they have to register as lobbyists? No, says Lansley. The bill covers consultant lobbying. Cheryl Gillan, the Conservative former Welsh secretary, says she is concerned that the bill could silence groups campaigning against the HS2 high speed rail project. Lansley says groups will still be able to campaign. But if an anti-HS2 group were trying to ensure that particular candidates lose, it would be covered by existing legislation. James Duddridge, a Conservative, says at the last election the third-party organisation that spent the most was Unison. That is why Labour are so angry, he says. Lansley says Duddridge has a point. Angela Eagle, the shadow leader of the Commons, quotes from the Electoral Commission's briefing. (See 10.32am.) She says this shows that Lansley's "complacency" is unjustified. Lansley says he had a meeting with the National Council for Voluntary Organisations yesterday. They have their own legal advice on the impact of the bill But that shows that the uncertainties supposedly in the new bill are actually in current legislation, he says. Here's a chart from the House of Commons library note (see 12.48pm) showing what impact the new rules would have on third-party groups that got involved in election campaigning in 2010. Tracey Crouch, a Conservative, says she used to work as a lobbyist, in the same firm as John Bercow. Can Lansley explain how the bill will not gag charities? Lansley says that the government has no intention of changing the boundaries governing what charities can do. Labour's Glenda Jackson interrupts. She says the bill has created a "firestorm" in her constituencies. People are angry about charities being silenced. Lansley says the things charities are worried about will not even happen. At the last election only two charities engaged in election-relating campaigning. Charities will still be free to campaign. The bill only affects election-related activities, he says. Andrew Lansley, the leader of the Commons, is opening the debate. Labour's Tom Clarke asks if the government will publish the results of its consultation on the bill. Lansley says the government consulted on the lobbying aspects of the bill. Labour did not even respond, he says. The second reading debate on the lobbying bill is starting. For reference, here is the Commons library note explaining in detail what the bill will do (pdf). • The Election Commission has strongly criticised the government's plans to tighten the rules on what charities can spend on campaigning in an election period. Jenny Watson made her comments in evidence to the political and constitutional reform committee ahead of this afternoon's second reading debate on the lobbying bill. Our view is that the bill as drafted creates a high level of uncertainty which will make it difficult for campaigners to be confident about how their activity from May onwards will be affected, while simultaneously imposing significant new burdens on such organisations. The wide definition of 'election purposes' gives us a great deal of discretion to interpret what activity will be regulated as political campaigning. This is discretion which we did not seek. It is likely that some of our readings of the law will be contentious and challenged, creating more uncertainty for those affected. This is made more likely by the change in our remit which the bill also makes and which again we did not seek. Some of the new controls in the bill may be impossible to enforce and Parliament will want carefully to consider what the changes might achieve in reality and balance this against the new burdens which the bill imposes. Andrew Lansley, the leader of the Commons, told the committee that the government did not believe the bill would have a significant impact on charities and that the government was just legislating to ensure that in future third-party spending could not distort the results of an election. But Graham Allen, the Labour chair of the committee, said the bill should be shelved for six months to allow it to be redrafted. Charity organisations have condemned the proposals in strong terms. And some lobbying groups are unhappy with the proposals on lobbying. Today the Public Relations Consultants Association said the bill should be dropped or rewritten. (See 11.13am.) And yesterday Jane Wilson, chief executive of the Chartered Institute of Public Relations, also criticised the plans. We are completely in favour of well researched and thought out ways of increasing transparency in political processes in order to build a greater level of trust in our democracy, but this bill is very poor and will not achieve that outcome ... The government should go back to the drawing board rather than try to force this legislation through in the face of good advice from organisations including the NCVO and the Electoral Commission. • Michael Gove, the education secretary, has criticised Labour MPs for cheering when the government lost the vote on Syria last week at the same time as news came through of an attack on children. (See 9.57am.) The Labour MP Michael Dugher said Gove's remarks were "grossly offensive". • Boris Johnson, the Conservative mayor of London, has renewed his call for David Cameron not to rule out a second Commons vote on attacking Syria. I have been very hostile and sceptical about British involvement in Syria, we all remember what happened in Iraq. But if you see someone gassing innocent civilians, that is a chilling, chilling memory for people in Europe of what happened in the 1930s and 1940s and of the cost of doing nothing. What I have said is if it can be proved that it really was the Assad regime that was responsible, and there's more proof, there's more evidence coming in, and if the Americans - who after all are the only real power that has the men and the material to deliver any retribution - can come up with a plan that is coherent, that is limited, that is punitive in some way but does not actually engage the West in some long-term commitment in a Syrian conflict, then I think there is a case for Britain looking again at whether or not we should participate. • George Osborne, the chancellor, has said there would be "very significant" tax rises or cuts to public spending if a Scottish oil fund was set up post-independence. He spoke as the Treasury published a fresh report on the economic impact of independence (pdf). • The Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development has sharply increased its growth forecast for the UK. As the Press Association reports, the OECD is predicting 1.5% growth in the UK in 2013, up from a forecast of 0.8% issued in May. It said: The pace of recovery in the major advanced economies improved in the second quarter and growth is expected to be maintained at a similar rate in the second half of the year. Activity is expanding at encouraging rates in North America, Japan and the United Kingdom, while the euro area as a whole is no longer in recession. • David Cameron has said that 93 new free schools are opening this month. • The Local Government Association has said that almost half of England's school districts will have more primary pupils than places within two years. Graham Allen goes next. Q: What is the purpose of part 3 of the bill? (Part 1 deals with lobbying, part 2 with third-party election spending, and part 3 tightens the rules about unions having to keep a register of their members.) Lansley says the government wants to make sure that parties have reliable information about their members. Q: Why not do that for political parties too? Lansley says union membership figures are relevant to decisions relating to votes in strike ballots and the operation of political funds. Those arguments do not apply to political parties, he says. And that's it. The session is over. Lansley will be up again in the Commons in about half an hour. I'll post a summary in a moment. Stephen Williams takes over again. Q: Have you been surprised by the depth of opposition to part 2 of the bill? Lansley says he is "disappointed", but not entirely surprised. People often over-characterise what government legislation will do, because that creates a row and gets them into the paper. But this bill will not stop groups campaign. It won't even stop 38 Degrees. Perhaps it might be nice to constrain 38 Degrees, he says. But the bill won't stop what they do. Q: I met 38 Degrees yesterday. They think the bill will constrain what they do. Why does the bill cut allowable spending by third-party groups so much? Lansley says the new limits will not affect many groups, but it will stop third-party groups spending more on election campaigning in future. Lansley says in the last session of parliament 13 measures were subject to pre-legislative scrutiny. But not everything can be subject to pre-legislative scrutiny. And, as the election gets closer, that gets harder for the government. Graham Allen steps in. Q: The lobbying bill was going to be about lobbying. The committee published a report. The government delayed responding for a year. Then suddenly the government publishes a bill with new sections that were added by surprise. Why was that? Lansley says that because the government had not announced anything, that does not mean work was not going on behind the scenes. The government never said it would introduce a bill dealing just with lobbying. Bills often end up including more than one part, he says. The three parts of this bill are connected, he says. The government consulted on its plans for lobbyists. But that does not mean the government should not be allowed to introduce other measures in the bill. Q: You are not winning many friends. This seems intended to alienate the groups affected by this. Lansley says he shares "a sense of regret" for the government's failure to consult with the committee. But there is substantial time available in the Commons to debate the bill. (The committee stage will take three days next week, with the debate taking place on the floor of the Commons because it is a constitutional bill.) Stephen Williams, the Lib Dem MP, is asking questions now. Q: Why was the bill not included in the Queen's Speech? Lansley says the proposals were not ready at that point. But it is normal to introduce bills in a session not included in the Queen's Speech. Q: Whose idea was part 2 (the section dealing with charities)? The government's, says Lansley, He will not elaborate. He says the government decided to legislate because it was concerned that third-party spending during elections could over time increase relative to spending by political parties, possibly distorting the result in some constituencies. Q: So you planned to stir up a gale of protests from charities? Lansley says he is an old hand. Groups complain about government. Q: This is an atrocity on a par with the Dangerous Dogs Act. Will you apologise for time it took responding to his committee? Lansley says his colleagues have already apologised to the committee about that. But the government has engaged in more pre-legislative scrutiny than any previous government. Paul Flynn, the Labour MP, goes next.
